## E1 — Synthetic events crash under a Tampermonkey @grant sandbox
- Tags: [browser][tampermonkey][events]
- What: `realClick`, the synthetic pointer/mouse click for node selection + picker interaction.
- Why built: Drive ProseMirror multi-select + the voice control like a real user.
- Design: One options object with `view: window`, dispatch pointerdown/mousedown/pointerup/mouseup/click.
- Got right: The event sequence and ctrl/meta modifiers were the right approach.
- Where/why it failed: With `@grant` (GM_setClipboard) the script is sandboxed and `window` is
  Tampermonkey's wrapper. Chrome's PointerEvent constructor rejects it: "Failed to read the
  'view' property from 'UIEventInit': Failed to convert value to 'Window'." The first event threw,
  so no click ever landed — every downstream symptom traced here.
- Fix: `@grant unsafeWindow`; `REAL_WINDOW = unsafeWindow || document.defaultView || window` used
  as `view`; a `mkEvent` helper retries construction without `view` if it still throws.
- Why this fix: The real Window is what the constructor requires; the fallback makes the click
  sequence crash-proof against this class of error.
- Rule of thumb: In a sandboxed userscript, never pass the sandbox `window` as an event `view`;
  use the real Window — and verify a click dispatches before blaming selectors.

## E2 — Picker detected by existence, not visibility
- Tags: [browser][radix][popover]
- What: Opening the voice picker to scrape/click voice rows.
- Why built: Assign a voice to a multi-selected group of nodes.
- Design: Record all matching containers before clicking the indicator, then accept any "new" one.
- Got right: Indicator selector and click target were correct.
- Where/why it failed: The picker (Radix popover) is pre-rendered hidden and merely toggled
  visible, so it was already in the "known" set and filtered out as not-new → looked unopened.
- Fix: Snapshot the set of *visible* containers before the click; accept any that become visible after.
- Why this fix: For popover frameworks the meaningful event is a visibility transition, not insertion.
- Rule of thumb: Detect popovers/menus by visibility change, not by node creation.

## E3 — Editor "clear" left stale nodes → plan/node misalignment
- Tags: [editor][prosemirror]
- What: Inserting clean text before assigning voices.
- Why built: One TTS node per paragraph, aligned to a per-paragraph voice plan.
- Design: focus → `execCommand('selectAll'+'delete')` → synthetic paste → map plan[i]→nodes[i].
- Got right: Synthetic paste reused Studio's own paragraph splitting; pasted counts matched plan.
- Where/why it failed: The clear left N default nodes at the front, so pasted nodes started at
  index N and every plan[i]→nodes[i] mapping shifted by N.
- Fix: Align assignment + verification to the TRAILING planLength nodes; retry the clear.
- Why this fix: Freshly pasted nodes are always last; trailing-alignment is robust to leftover
  leading nodes without needing a perfect clear.
- Rule of thumb: Never assume a rich-text "clear" emptied the editor; align by position and verify counts.

## E4 — Silent failure hid the root cause
- Tags: [process][observability]
- What: First versions of the assignment flow.
- Why built: Drive the editor/picker via synthetic events.
- Design: v0.1 ran the click/picker pipeline with no error handling.
- Got right: Pipeline structure (parse → insert → resolve → assign → verify); parser/planner.
- Where/why it failed: A throw in the first synthetic click (E1) vanished silently, so
  symptoms ("picker didn't open") pointed at the wrong layers for two iterations.
- Fix: Added try/catch/finally + a structured debug report/log (v0.2), surfacing the real stack.
- Why this fix: Observability turns an invisible failure into a precise diagnosis (and doubles
  as the self-grading mechanism).
- Rule of thumb: Instrument first; verify the lowest-level primitive before theorizing upward.

## E10 — Harness blocks pushing to external remotes regardless of auth
- Tags: [process][harness][git]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Pushing the finished Lockstep repo to its GitHub remote from the agent session.
- Why built: Ship the work to `github.com/PappyDaPimp/lockstep`.
- Design: Run `git push` to the external GitHub remote from inside the agent's execution environment.
- Got right: Local commits, branch, and remote config were all correct.
- Where/why it failed: The harness hard-blocks pushing to any GitHub remote outside the original
  execution environment — regardless of permission rules or the user's explicit authorization. The
  push is refused at the environment layer, not by git or GitHub, so no credential/config fix helps.
- Fix: Don't fight it — generate a `.bat` (or shell script) containing the push commands and hand it
  to the user to run on their own machine; they drive the last mile.
- Why this fix: The block is environment policy, not a fixable auth problem; routing the push through
  the user's local machine is the only reliable path.
- Rule of thumb: If a push to an external remote is refused no matter the auth, it's the
  harness/environment blocking it — hand the user a script to push locally instead of retrying.
- Provenance: verified
- Link: [redacted]

## E6 — Accent styling silently stopped applying after the first paragraph
- Tags: [tts][voice][prompt-engineering]
- Date: 2026-06-14
- What: The bracket-tag protocol that drives Zane's exaggerated Japanese-accent "bits" in the TTS scripts.
- Why built: To make the text-to-speech engine reliably switch into the accent for a bit and reliably
  return to the normal host voice, across a multi-paragraph script.
- Design: An accent bit is opened with `[thick exaggerated Japanese accent] … [normal voice]`, one tag
  per bracket, and the accent tag is re-declared at the start of every paragraph that needs it.
- Got right: One-tag-per-bracket plus an explicit `[normal voice]` exit kept the accent from bleeding
  into surrounding host dialogue; the convention generalized cleanly across all eight scripts.
- Where/why it failed: A single accent tag placed once at the top stopped applying after the first
  paragraph — the engine resets voice/style state at paragraph boundaries, so later paragraphs silently
  reverted to the default voice with no visible error in the script.
- Fix: Re-declare the accent tag in every paragraph that should carry it, and close each bit explicitly
  with `[normal voice]`; documented this in `ACCENT_RULES.md`.
- Why this fix: It adapts to the engine's actual per-paragraph state reset rather than assuming styling
  is sticky; an explicit exit tag is more robust than relying on the next tag to override a lingering state.
- Rule of thumb: If a styling directive silently stops applying partway through, assume the renderer
  resets state at block boundaries and re-declare it per block.
- Link: [redacted]

## E5 — TTS engine accepts-but-ignores certain bracket tags (silent no-op)
- Tags: [tts][voice][prompt-engineering]
- Date: 2026-06-14
- What: A maintained "dead-tags" list of bracket emotion/style tags the TTS engine accepts but does
  not act on (e.g. `[building]`).
- Why built: To stop authors from spending tags that produce no audible change and to constrain scripts
  to directives that actually work.
- Design: Scripts draw only from a known-good set of emotional-state tags; tags found to have no effect
  are catalogued as "dead" in `ACCENT_RULES.md` and avoided everywhere.
- Got right: Centralizing the deadlist in the shared ruleset meant every script reused the same guard
  instead of each rediscovering the same dud tags.
- Where/why it failed: Certain plausible-looking tags (e.g. `[building]`) are parsed without error but
  produce no change in the audio — a silent no-op that looks correct on the page and only reveals itself
  on listen-back.
- Fix: Catalogue the ineffective tags as "dead" and exclude them, preferring the validated set; while
  drafting Segment 2 this caught a would-be `[building]` cue before it shipped.
- Why this fix: A silent no-op cannot be caught by reading the script, so an explicit deny-list is the
  only durable safeguard.
- Rule of thumb: When a system silently accepts-but-ignores some inputs, keep an explicit allow/deny
  list — silent no-ops never surface themselves.
- Link: [redacted]

## E1 — `
` is a silent no-op in SSML - Tags: [ssml][elevenlabs][text-processing] - Date: 2026-06-14 - What: The blank-verse SSML file (`the_wardens_story_verse_ssml.txt`), where each verse line carries a trailing pause tag. - Why built: ElevenLabs needs explicit `` tags to reproduce verse lineation as audible pauses; punctuation alone would not preserve the line rhythm. - Design: Append `` to each line end, with longer breaks at stanza and section boundaries. - Got right: The per-line break approach and the graduated timing (short at line, longer at stanza, longest at the three closing questions). - Where/why it failed: While drafting, several enjambed lines received `
` instead of a break tag — an HTML reflex leaking into an SSML file. ElevenLabs does not honor `
`, so those line boundaries would have rendered with no pause. The error was invisible when reading the text as prose; it existed only at the tag level. - Fix: `sed -i 's///g'` across the file, then `grep` to confirm zero `
` remained. - Why this fix: It normalized every line boundary to the single pause primitive the engine reads, making the markup uniform rather than relying on spotting each stray tag by eye. - Rule of thumb: In SSML, `` is the only pause primitive; treat any `
` as a silent failure and grep for it before shipping. - Provenance (not verified): not verified - Link: [redacted]

## E1 — Shared midpoint camera framed nothing when players spawned apart
- Tags: [gamedev][coop][camera]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Operative spawn placement + the shared midpoint-follow camera at level start.
- Why built: Co-op start with one camera that frames both players.
- Design: `spawnPoints[0]`/`[1]` placed P1/P2 at opposite arena corners; the single camera follows the
  midpoint of the two operatives.
- Got right: The midpoint follow keeps both framed whenever they're near each other.
- Where/why it failed: On launch the operatives sat at opposite far corners, so the midpoint was the
  empty arena center — the camera framed neither. User reported "no player mobs on screen."
- Fix: Spawn P1/P2 adjacent (side-by-side) in `MapBuilder` spawnPoints.
- Why this fix: A co-op game should literally begin together; adjacent spawns keep the shared camera
  valid until players choose to separate (split-screen handles real separation).
- Rule of thumb: In shared-camera co-op, spawn players together — a midpoint camera frames nothing if
  they start apart.
- Link: [redacted]

## E2 — Tether cleanup was asymmetric when a partner went down
- Tags: [coop][state]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Tether mechanic (freeze + spring-pull one operative toward the other).
- Why built: Let a player reel themselves to their partner to regroup.
- Design: `op.tetheredTo = partner.id`; each frame a spring sets the tetherer's velocity toward the
  partner; the partner's own tether is cleared in `goDown()`.
- Got right: Symmetric toggle and break-on-move-input worked.
- Where/why it failed: When the partner went down, only the downed one's `tetheredTo` was cleared — the
  live tetherer kept being reeled into the downed body. Asymmetric cleanup.
- Fix: In the spring loop, if `partner.isDown`, clear `op.tetheredTo` and skip.
- Why this fix: Tether is meaningless toward a downed partner; clearing at the point of use covers every
  path a partner can go down without scattering cleanup across events.
- Rule of thumb: Clear a relationship at the point you act on it, not only at the event that ends one side.
- Link: [redacted]

## E3 — A tracked stat had a reader but no writer
- Tags: [stats][process]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Per-level "revives given" stat shown on the between-level screen.
- Why built: Feed the skill-point award and the stats display.
- Design: Counter on `Operative`, reset each level, rendered in the Safe House.
- Got right: Declared, reset, and displayed correctly.
- Where/why it failed: Nothing ever incremented it — the revive-completion path restored HP and played a
  sound but never bumped the reviver's counter, so it always read 0.
- Fix: Increment `reviver.revivesGiven` when `addReviveProgress()` reports completion.
- Why this fix: The completion branch is the single source of truth for "a revive happened"; the write
  belongs there, not in the UI.
- Rule of thumb: A tracked stat needs a writer at its event, not just a reader at its display.
- Link: [redacted]

## E4 — One input edge consumed by two systems
- Tags: [input][coop]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: The "interact" button shared by revive, escort drop/pass, and shop purchase.
- Why built: One contextual action button.
- Design: Each system independently edge-detected the same `interact` intent.
- Got right: Each behavior worked in isolation.
- Where/why it failed: Standing in a shop zone while carrying a payload and pressing interact fired both
  the escort drop and a shop buy in the same frame — two systems consuming one input edge.
- Fix: Shop ignores the press while `isCarrying`; then buy was fully decoupled onto its own action
  (X gamepad / F keyboard) with a device-aware prompt.
- Why this fix: Overloading one button across contexts needs explicit priority or separation; a
  dedicated buy action removes the ambiguity entirely.
- Rule of thumb: If two systems read the same input edge, one will fire when you don't want it — gate by
  context or give it its own button.
- Link: [redacted]

## E5 — One-shot pickup re-fired during its despawn fade
- Tags: [phaser][physics]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: COLLECT-objective shard pickup on overlap.
- Why built: Count collected shards toward the objective.
- Design: `overlap(shardGroup, operative)` → increment, play a 140ms fade tween, then destroy.
- Got right: Overlap detection was correct.
- Where/why it failed: `collect()` started the fade but left the shard active with its body enabled, so
  the overlap kept firing every frame during the fade — 8 shards counted as ~10.
- Fix: On collect, immediately `setActive(false)` + disable the body (guarded against re-entry), then
  run the fade tween.
- Why this fix: The "collected" flag must flip on the same frame as the trigger; visual teardown can
  lag, collision state cannot.
- Rule of thumb: Deactivate a one-shot pickup the instant it's taken — never let a despawn animation
  gate further hits.
- Link: [redacted]

## E6 — Backgrounded tab froze the loop; huge deltas caused tunneling
- Tags: [phaser][physics][process]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Phaser game-loop timing under backgrounded/occluded tabs.
- Why built: Reliable runtime verification plus robust play on focus loss.
- Design: Default RAF-driven loop + Arcade physics.
- Got right: Fine while the tab was focused.
- Where/why it failed: Backgrounded, RAF paused (`loop.time` froze, nothing updated); switching to
  setTimeout then produced huge per-frame deltas, so fast projectiles tunneled through bodies and
  overlaps silently missed.
- Fix: `fps: { forceSetTimeOut: true, min: 30 }` + `disableVisibilityChange: true` in gameConfig — loop
  keeps ticking when occluded, and delta is clamped (~33ms) so a hitch slows the sim instead of tunneling.
- Why this fix: Clamping max delta is the general cure for tunneling under frame hitches; the
  timeout/visibility flags just keep the loop alive for headless testing and alt-tab play.
- Rule of thumb: Cap your max frame delta — without it, any stall turns into missed collisions.
- Link: [redacted]

## E7 — Vite HMR stacked Phaser.Game instances without a dispose hook
- Tags: [phaser][vite][tooling]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Vite hot-module-reload during development.
- Why built: Fast iteration.
- Design: `main.ts` constructs `new Phaser.Game(config)` at import time.
- Got right: Cold loads were fine.
- Where/why it failed: Every hot update re-ran `main.ts` and created another `Phaser.Game` without
  disposing the old one — stacked canvases/loops, and debug `window.game` pointed at a different
  instance than the visible one, yielding impossible-looking state during verification.
- Fix: `if (import.meta.hot) import.meta.hot.dispose(() => game.destroy(true))`.
- Why this fix: An engine that owns a canvas + RAF loop must be torn down on hot-dispose, or instances
  accumulate.
- Rule of thumb: With HMR, anything that grabs a global resource (canvas, loop, socket) needs an
  explicit dispose hook.
- Link: [redacted]

## E8 — Phaser overlap callback args aren't positionally ordered
- Tags: [phaser][physics]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Arcade-physics overlap callbacks for projectile↔enemy↔operative collisions.
- Why built: Apply damage/effects on contact.
- Design: Registered overlaps and read the callback args positionally (`bolt` = first arg, target = second).
- Got right: Group-vs-group cases happened to line up.
- Where/why it failed: `overlap(group, sprite)` does NOT guarantee (object1, object2) order — for
  operative overlaps the args came back swapped, so `bolt` was actually the Operative and
  `bolt.deactivate()` threw; the exception aborted `world.step` and the rest of `update()` every frame,
  freezing camera/AI/everything with no surfaced console error.
- Fix: A type-based resolver `pick(a, b, Ctor)` (`core/overlap.ts`) used in every overlap callback;
  never index by position.
- Why this fix: Resolving participants by `instanceof` is order-independent and immune to Phaser's
  internal arg ordering.
- Rule of thumb: Never trust positional args in a physics overlap callback — resolve each participant by type.
- Link: [redacted]

## E9 — A help-multiplier became a penalty at the hard end
- Tags: [gamedesign]
- Date: 2026-06-15
- What: Objective-count difficulty model `count = floor(base · f^k)`.
- Why built: Scale per-level workload by difficulty without making combined-objective levels overwhelming.
- Design: `f` = difficulty factor (easy 0.8 / normal 1.0 / hard 1.2); `k` = combo exponent (1 for a
  single objective, 2 when combined), capped at 2.
- Got right: Applying the factor twice for combos keeps total workload bounded as objective types stack.
- Where/why it failed: A naive symmetric `f^k` makes Hard combos explode (×1.44) — penalizing exactly
  the players who opt into harder multi-objective levels, an unintended difficulty cliff.
- Fix: Make the combo exponent one-way — `k` only applies when `f < 1` (Easy thins combos); at
  Normal/Hard the combo multiplier collapses to `f^1`.
- Why this fix: The combo adjustment was intended as mercy on Easy, not a surcharge on Hard; gating it
  on `f < 1` preserves the intent and removes the cliff.
- Rule of thumb: A multiplier meant to help should be clamped so it can't silently become a penalty at
  the other end of the range.
- Link: [redacted]

## E1 — data driven content needs a validation ladder
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][gamedesign][tooling]
- What: Made the RPG fully data-driven — items, enemies, quests, NPCs, archetypes all live in `src/data/*.json`; the code knows only *shapes* (an item has a slot; a quest has typed objectives), never specific content. Adding content is a JSON edit, not a code change.
- Why built: To keep authoring cheap and unbounded and to keep the dangerous surface (new mechanics) small and in reviewed code. The deliberate code/content boundary sits at *types*: a new quest is data; a new *kind* of objective is one reducer case.
- Design: A loader builds id-keyed maps from the JSON; cross-file references are by id (`loot[].itemId`, quest `giver`, `rewards.items`, archetype `startingItems`).
- Got right: Authoring a new enemy or quest needs zero code and is referenceable immediately; the small mechanic surface stays reviewable.
- Where/why it failed: Going data-driven silently trades away compile-time safety. A single typo in an item id ships a quest whose `collect` objective can never be satisfied — and nothing errors at load. The failure moves from the compiler (where you'd catch it instantly) to the player (who hits an uncompletable quest). With hand-edited JSON this is a *when*, not an *if*.
- Fix: Build a validation ladder, cheapest-first: (1) JSON Schema per content type to catch malformed records; (2) a referential-integrity + completability check — every referenced id resolves, and every quest is actually completable (its kill/collect targets exist and spawn/drop somewhere); (3) run a headless end-to-end smoke playthrough in CI that exercises the live content graph and fails loudly on a broken reference.
- Why this fix: The compiler used to be the validation step; once content leaves code, you must rebuild that safety net deliberately or it doesn't exist. A headless smoke test is the highest-value rung because it catches semantic breakage (uncompletable quest) that schema/ref checks alone miss.
- Rule of thumb: The moment you make content data-driven, you have opted out of compile-time safety for that content — add a validation step (schema → referential integrity → headless smoke playthrough) in the same change, or breakage relocates from the compiler to the player.
- Provenance (verified/assumed): assumed
- Link: [redacted]

## E2 — deterministic rng as save and coop seam
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][state][coop]
- What: Building the authoritative simulation core for a 3D RPG vertical slice ("Echoes of the Shattered Realm"). Decided that all randomness in the sim must flow through a single seeded RNG whose entire state is a `{seed, count}` pair stored *inside* the saved world object — `Math.random()` is banned in the sim layer.
- Why built: Needed save/load, and wanted to keep lockstep co-op possible later without a rewrite. Both wants turned out to be the *same* requirement: a reproducible random stream.
- Design: One mulberry32 generator; `nextFloat` advances `rng.count`; the world holds `rng:{seed,count}`. Save = `JSON.stringify(world)`; load = `JSON.parse`. Content (items/enemies/quests) is NOT serialized — it is reloaded from data and rebound, so content can be patched under old saves.
- Got right: A mid-run save/load round-trip is asserted in the headless demo — after reload, `rng.count` is preserved and the next roll is identical to the un-saved timeline. One discipline bought three things: exact save/load, replay-from-seed for repro bug reports, and the lockstep-co-op option.
- Where/why it failed: The non-obvious trap is everything that *silently* breaks determinism and won't show up until a save desyncs or two clients diverge: an ambient `Math.random()`, a `Date.now()`/`new Date()` leaking into game logic, or unordered iteration over a hash map affecting outcome order. None of these error — they just quietly make the stream irreproducible.
- Fix: Route every roll through the one seeded RNG; ban `Math.random()` in the sim (a lint rule is the cheap enforcement); keep wall-clock time out of the sim (advance an in-world clock via commands instead); iterate entities in a stable id order when order affects outcomes.
- Why this fix: Determinism is a global property — a single ambient call anywhere in the sim voids it. Centralizing the entropy source and forbidding ambient sources is the only way to keep the guarantee provable rather than hoped-for.
- Rule of thumb: If you want save/load to round-trip exactly OR lockstep multiplayer later, store the RNG state inside the saved state and forbid ambient `Math.random()`/`Date.now()` in the simulation. The constraint it forces — float-nondeterministic physics must stay OUT of the authoritative layer — is the tell that you're doing it right.
- Provenance (verified/assumed): assumed
- Link: [redacted]

## E3 — gamepad active polling and input parity
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [input][gamedev][harness]
- What: Adding Xbox/standard gamepad support (USB + Bluetooth) to a web game via the Gamepad API, including menu/start-screen navigation.
- Why built: Controller parity with touch/keyboard, and the start screen + pause menu have selectable options that must be navigable without a mouse.
- Design: A per-frame `pollGamepad(dt)` reading axes/buttons into the shared command vocabulary, plus an overlay-navigation branch for menus; device detection toggled body classes that swap the on-screen UI.
- Got right: Routing all devices into one command vocabulary so gameplay actions mapped cleanly; hiding the on-screen touch buttons when a pad connects.
- Where/why it failed: Two gaps made it "not work" for the user. (1) I keyed pad presence off the `gamepadconnected` event only — but Chrome does not expose a pad (or fire the event) until the user presses a button, and re-navigating to a fresh screen could leave `padIndex` unset, so menus saw no controller at all. (2) Menu navigation only listened to the D-pad (buttons 12–15); users naturally push the left *stick*, and some controllers report the D-pad as a hat axis, so nothing moved the selection.
- Fix: (1) Actively scan `navigator.getGamepads()` every frame and adopt the first connected pad (don't rely on the event); clear it if it vanishes. (2) Accept BOTH the left stick (with a debounce so one push = one step) and the D-pad for menu navigation, and let A activate + Start close. Also unlabeled UI: on-screen buttons must be readable (words, not glyphs) and controls shown, or players press random buttons.
- Why this fix: The Gamepad API is poll-based and gesture-gated; the connect event is a convenience, not a reliable source of truth — poll it. And input parity means every device can drive every surface (menus included), which requires supporting each device's natural affordance, not just one canonical mapping.
- Rule of thumb: Detect gamepads by polling `getGamepads()` each frame, not by trusting `gamepadconnected` (it only fires after a button press). And make menus navigable by BOTH stick and D-pad — plus label on-screen buttons with words, or players can't tell what does what.
- Provenance (verified/assumed): verified
- Link: [redacted]

## E4 — realtime combat over turnbased reducer
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][state][gamedesign]
- What: Adding real-time 3D combat on top of an authoritative reducer that was originally written turn-based (one ATTACK command resolved a full exchange: player hit + enemy retaliation), without breaking the deterministic golden replay test that pins that turn-based behavior.
- Why built: The 3D client needs the player to strike on a cooldown and enemies to hit independently on their own cadence — not a synchronous exchange. But the headless demo/replay depend on the exchange semantics for their fixed RNG fingerprint.
- Design: Two moves. (1) Parametrize, don't fork: ATTACK gained an optional `retaliate` flag defaulting to true. The headless paths pass nothing → identical RNG draws → golden fingerprint unchanged; the real-time client passes `retaliate:false` (player damage only) and drives enemy damage through a new `ENEMY_STRIKE` command on a renderer-owned cooldown. (2) i-frames without sim changes: a dodge's invulnerability window is implemented by the renderer simply NOT issuing `ENEMY_STRIKE` while the dodge is active — no "invulnerable" state in the sim at all.
- Got right: One reducer, one combat code path, golden test preserved; enemy positions/cooldowns live in the renderer (cosmetic) so determinism holds; dodge i-frames fell out of the architecture for free.
- Where/why it failed: The naive first instinct — change ATTACK to drop retaliation for real-time — would have silently shifted every RNG draw in the headless replay and broken the golden fingerprint (or forced rebaselining it, losing its regression value). And the instinct to add an `invulnerable` flag to the player would have put a renderer-only concern into saved sim state.
- Fix: Default-true flag + a separate enemy command; i-frames as command-withholding in the renderer.
- Why this fix: Extending a reducer for a new timing model is safest as an *optional* behavior toggle (old callers unchanged) rather than a semantic change. And the authoritative-command boundary means "don't take damage" is best expressed as "don't issue the damage command," keeping transient combat state out of the save.
- Rule of thumb: To run real-time over a turn-based authoritative reducer, make the turn-based "reaction" an opt-out flag (default preserves old behavior + tests) and add a separate command for the independent actor; express invulnerability windows by withholding the damage command, not by adding state to the sim.
- Provenance (verified/assumed): assumed
- Link: [redacted]

## E5 — renderer mutated sim state one char slip
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][state][camera]
- What: Building the 3D render spine (Milestone 1) on top of an authoritative-sim architecture whose load-bearing rule is "the renderer reads world state but NEVER mutates it; all mutation goes through reduce()."
- Why built: Keeping the renderer read-only is what preserves save/load and the co-op seam (a server owns the world; clients only render). It is the single most important boundary in the design.
- Design: Camera-follow code computes a camera position from the player's world position each frame.
- Got right: The boundary held everywhere else — every gameplay mutation (move, travel, quests, combat) routes through reduce(); this was the lone exception, which is exactly why it stood out on a diff re-read.
- Where/why it failed: In a camera helper I wrote `camera.position.y = Math.max(2, p.y = 3 + off.y)` — an accidental assignment (`p.y = ...`) that wrote a new `y` field onto the player's authoritative position object from inside the renderer. Caught only by re-reading the diff, not by any tool.
- Fix: Removed the assignment; the camera reads `p.x`/`p.z` and derives its own height locally. The renderer holds its own view state (yaw/pitch/offset); it never assigns to anything under `world`.
- Why this fix: The boundary only holds if it's enforced, and a one-character slip (`=` where you meant to read) defeats it without any signal. The durable cure is a guard, not vigilance: freeze the sim state the renderer receives (Object.freeze / a readonly proxy) in dev, or a lint rule banning assignment to `world.*` outside src/sim, so the slip fails loudly instead of silently.
- Rule of thumb: When one layer is "read-only" over another's state, enforce it mechanically (freeze the object or lint the boundary) — a single stray `=` mutates shared state with no error and no symptom until a save desyncs.

## E7 — vendoring split esm lib and headless webgl
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][tooling][vite]
- What: Shipping a Three.js render spine to GitHub Pages with no bundler — the site is plain static HTML + ESM + JSON — and verifying it headless in CI/Playwright.
- Why built: A bundler-free static site is the simplest thing that deploys on Pages and is byte-identical to what runs locally and in the headless test. Two non-obvious things bit during setup.
- Design: Vendor Three.js into the repo (`web/vendor/`) and `import * as THREE from "./vendor/three.module.js"`; verify the page in headless Chromium via Playwright.
- Got right: Vendoring instead of a CDN was the right call — it made the site self-contained on Pages and the headless test hermetic, which is precisely what surfaced both issues locally instead of in production.
- Where/why it failed: (1) Three's ESM build is SPLIT: `three.module.js` is a thin entry that does `import { ... } from './three.core.js'`. Vendoring only the named entry file gives a runtime 404 on the sibling and a blank page — you must vendor the whole sibling set, not just the file you import. (2) Headless Chromium returns a NULL WebGL context by default on modern builds, so the renderer silently fails to draw and a naive "did it load?" test passes while rendering nothing.
- Fix: (1) Copy both `three.module.js` AND `three.core.js` into `web/vendor/` (they sit together; the relative import resolves). (2) Launch headless Chromium with `--enable-unsafe-swiftshader --use-gl=angle --use-angle=swiftshader --ignore-gpu-blocklist` to get a real software WebGL context, and additionally make the app degrade gracefully (keep the update loop, skip only `renderer.render`) so logic stays verifiable even where GL is absent.
- Why this fix: A split-entry library is only "vendored" when every file its import graph touches is present; copying the named entry alone is a partial copy that looks complete. And SwiftShader flags are the documented way to exercise real WebGL in headless CI — without them you get false-green "it loaded" tests.
- Rule of thumb: When you vendor an ESM library by hand, follow its import graph and copy every sibling it pulls (entry files are often thin re-export shells); and to truly test WebGL headless, enable SwiftShader explicitly (`--enable-unsafe-swiftshader`) AND make rendering degrade gracefully so a missing GL context fails loud, not silent.

## E1 — benchmark data must exercise cost driver
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: local/sandbox-myers-diff
- Tags: [process][harness]
- What: Benchmarking a Myers diff, whose cost is O((N+M)·D) where D = edit distance (number of differences) — fast when inputs are similar, quadratic when very different.
- Why built: To confirm the algorithm's cost model empirically.
- Design: First benchmark diffed two "random" strings over an 8-character alphabet, expecting a large D and slow run.
- Got right: Eventually demonstrating the quadratic worst case.
- Where/why it failed: The first benchmark showed the "different" case as FAST (D≈16, ~1ms) and concluded cost was flat — wrong. With only 8 symbols, two random strings share a huge common subsequence, so D is small regardless of "randomness". The test data didn't exercise the cost driver (D); it accidentally measured the easy case and would have hidden a real performance cliff.
- Fix: Generate low-overlap inputs — unique tokens (`a0,a1,…` vs `b0,b1,…`) so the common subsequence is near-zero and D≈2N. Then the worst case is obvious: disjoint inputs went 91ms→289ms→2372ms as N doubled (super-linear), while similar inputs stayed ~flat (~0.1ms).
- Why this fix: A benchmark only means something if its inputs actually drive the variable that dominates cost. For diff that's D (dissimilarity), which is controlled by alphabet size/overlap, NOT by "is the string random". Choose test data that maximizes the cost driver, or you measure the wrong regime and ship a latent cliff.
- Rule of thumb: Benchmark inputs must exercise the algorithm's actual cost driver, not a proxy — for Myers diff that's edit distance D (use unique/low-overlap tokens), since a small alphabet forces high overlap and hides the quadratic worst case. Identify what dominates the complexity and construct data that maximizes it.

## E2 — esm module main guard
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: local/sandbox-myers-diff
- Tags: [tooling][process][harness]
- What: A Node ESM module (`myers.mjs`) that exported a function AND ran a block of inline tests ending in `process.exit(fail ? 1 : 0)` at the top level.
- Why built: Convenient to keep the algorithm and its quick tests in one runnable file.
- Design: `export function diff(){…}` plus a bare test section at module top level.
- Got right: Running the file directly (`node myers.mjs`) worked fine.
- Where/why it failed: When a SECOND file `import`ed the module to use `diff()`, the module's top-level test code executed on import — and its `process.exit()` terminated the whole process before the importer's own code ran. The importer's tests silently never executed; the output showed the *imported* module's tests instead, masquerading as the caller's.
- Fix: Guard the test/CLI block so it only runs when the file is the entrypoint, the ESM equivalent of Python's `if __name__ == "__main__"` / CommonJS `require.main === module`: `import { pathToFileURL } from "node:url"; if (process.argv[1] && import.meta.url === pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href) { …tests… }`.
- Why this fix: ESM has no implicit main check; any top-level statement runs on import. Side-effectful top-level code (tests, servers, `process.exit`) turns a library into a script that hijacks whoever imports it. The main-guard restores "library when imported, script when run".
- Rule of thumb: Never leave side-effectful top-level code (tests, `process.exit`, server start) unguarded in an importable ESM module — it runs on import and can kill the importer. Gate it behind an is-main check (`import.meta.url === pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href`).

## E2 — parsefloat silently truncates malformed numbers
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: local/sandbox-expr
- Tags: [process][tooling]
- What: Tokenizing numbers in a small expression parser with a greedy character class — consume runs of `[0-9.]`, then `parseFloat(slice)`.
- Why built: Simplest way to lex numeric literals including decimals.
- Design: `while (/[0-9.]/.test(s[j])) j++;` then `parseFloat(s.slice(i,j))`.
- Got right: Ordinary numbers (`3`, `1.5`, `.25`) tokenized fine.
- Where/why it failed: The character class also accepts MALFORMED numbers with multiple dots (`1.2.3`), and `parseFloat` doesn't reject them — it silently parses the valid prefix and stops (`parseFloat("1.2.3") === 1.2`). So `1.2.3` evaluated to `1.2` with no error. A silent-wrong result — the worst failure mode, because it looks like it worked.
- Fix: Validate the number token's shape rather than trusting the parser: either match a strict numeric pattern (`/^\d*\.\d+|\d+\.?\d*$/`, reject >1 dot) at lex time, or convert with `Number(slice)` (which returns `NaN` for `"1.2.3"`, so you can throw) instead of `parseFloat` (which truncates). Prefer the tool that REJECTS malformed input over the one that salvages a prefix.
- Why this fix: `parseFloat` is designed to be lenient (parse-a-prefix), which is exactly wrong for a tokenizer that must accept-or-reject a whole lexeme. `Number()`/strict regex fail loudly on garbage, turning a silent-wrong into a catchable error.
- Rule of thumb: A greedy `[0-9.]+` number lexer accepts malformed multi-dot numbers, and `parseFloat` silently truncates them (`"1.2.3"→1.2`) — validate the lexeme shape or use `Number()` (NaN-on-garbage), not `parseFloat` (prefix-salvage), so bad numeric input fails loudly instead of computing a wrong answer.

## E3 — property test diff by reconstruction
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: local/sandbox-myers-diff
- Tags: [harness][process]
- What: Testing a Myers diff implementation that outputs an edit script (keep/del/ins ops) between two sequences.

- Got right: Recognizing that asserting the EXACT op list is the wrong test.
- Where/why it failed: A minimal edit script is NOT unique — several different op sequences can share the same minimal edit count and all be correct. Golden-output assertions on the exact ops are brittle: a valid implementation change (or a different-but-equal path) fails the test though nothing is wrong.
- Fix: Property-test by RECONSTRUCTION. From the op list, rebuild both inputs: keep+ins must reproduce sequence B, and keep+del must reproduce sequence A. Assert both round-trips (and, separately, that the edit count is minimal where known). This checks *correctness of the transform* without pinning a specific path.
- Why this fix: The real contract of a diff is "these ops turn A into B (and drop the deletions to recover A)", not "these exact ops". Testing the invariant instead of the representation makes the test robust to legitimate non-determinism in the output.
- Rule of thumb: When an algorithm's output isn't unique (diffs, minimal paths, tokenizations, layouts), don't assert the exact output — assert the invariants it must satisfy (reconstruct the inputs, check the cost is minimal). Property/round-trip tests beat golden output for non-unique results.

## E2 — report the actual cycle not the leftover
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: local/sandbox-toposort
- Tags: [process][harness]
- What: Detecting and reporting a dependency cycle in a task/topological ordering.
- Why built: A scheduler must fail with a useful "these depend on each other" message when the graph isn't a DAG.
- Design: Kahn's algorithm (in-degree removal) already detects a cycle for free: whatever nodes it can't place are "unschedulable", so the first instinct is to report that leftover set as the cycle.
- Got right: Kahn correctly detects THAT a cycle exists.
- Where/why it failed: The leftover set OVER-REPORTS the cycle — it contains every node stuck in the cycle AND every node merely downstream of it (that can never reach in-degree 0). Reporting it as "the cycle" is misleading: e.g. for a→b→c→a plus a→d, the leftover is {a,b,c,d}, but d is not in the cycle at all. A user chasing the "cycle" wastes time on innocent nodes.
- Fix: To report the ACTUAL cycle, run a DFS with three-color marking (white/gray/black); a back-edge to a GRAY node (one still on the current recursion stack) is the cycle, and you extract the exact path by slicing the stack from that node. Use Kahn for fast *detection*; use DFS when you need to *name the cycle* in an error.
- Why this fix: In-degree removal loses the path structure, so it can say "unschedulable" but not "which loop". DFS keeps the active path on the stack, so the back-edge gives the minimal cycle directly — the thing a human actually needs to fix it.
- Rule of thumb: Kahn's "unschedulable leftover" detects a cycle but over-reports it (includes downstream-of-cycle nodes) — for an actionable error message, extract the real cycle with a DFS that flags a back-edge to a gray (on-stack) node and slices the path off the recursion stack.

- Where/why it failed: The tiles "ate" scroll drags — a finger starting on a tile couldn't scroll the page, because the elements captured the touch gesture as a tap/press instead of letting the browser pan.
- Fix: Set `touch-action: pan-y` on the tiles and menu rows so vertical panning is handed back to the browser while taps still register (and trim the default home to few enough cards to reduce the need to scroll over tiles).
- Why this fix: `touch-action` tells the browser which gestures it may handle natively; `pan-y` reserves vertical scrolling for the browser while leaving tap/click to the element. Without it, interactive elements can swallow the scroll.
- Rule of thumb: On mobile, interactive tiles/rows over a scrollable area need `touch-action: pan-y` (or `pan-x`) so they don't eat scroll drags — declare the axes the browser should still own. (Note the flip side: a full-screen game canvas wants `touch-action: none` to own ALL gestures — pick per element.)

## E6 — webaudio voice budget and teardown
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Dog
- Tags: [audio][process]
- What: A synthesized bark SFX that built 15–30 Web Audio nodes (oscillators, filters, shapers, gains) per trigger, spammable by the player.
- Why built: Rich layered synthesis makes a satisfying bark.
- Design: Each bark constructed its node graph on demand; the player could mash the bark key.
- Got right: The bark sounded great in isolation.
- Where/why it failed: Audio "struggled" under load — mashing bark created hundreds of live nodes with no rate limit, no voice cap, and no teardown, so the graph grew unbounded and playback degraded/crackled.
- Fix: Three guards together — a game-level bark COOLDOWN, a VOICE BUDGET (cap transient one-shots at ~16), and `onended` handlers that DISCONNECT a sound's entire node chain when it finishes so nodes are released.
- Why this fix: Web Audio nodes are not free and aren't garbage-collected while connected and scheduled; unbounded transient synthesis is a leak. Rate-limit at the source, cap concurrent voices, and explicitly tear down finished graphs.
- Rule of thumb: For frequently-triggered synthesized SFX, add a cooldown + a concurrent-voice cap + `onended` disconnect of the whole node chain — Web Audio nodes accumulate and degrade playback if you never rate-limit or tear them down.

## E8 — world rebuild through one clear respawn path
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][state][process]
- What: A 3D game whose authoritative `world` is rebuilt in several situations — character-archetype switch, and load-from-save — while the renderer holds derived scene objects (enemy meshes, villagers, lore motes) keyed to that world's entity ids.
- Why built: Switching class and loading a save both replace the world; the scene must follow.
- Design: Each rebuild path (archetype switch, load) did its OWN partial cleanup inline.
- Got right: The fresh-boot path worked.
- Where/why it failed: Two bugs from ad-hoc partial rebuilds. (1) The archetype switch cleared enemies but NOT the villagers/lore, so switching class twice DUPLICATED the villagers (leaked meshes accumulating). (2) The Load path replaced `world` but never rebuilt the enemies, so every enemy mesh still referenced the OLD world's entity ids — `world.entities[id]` was now undefined and the enemies froze (a stray-NaN-style desync, silent except for the earlier per-subsystem guard catching it).
- Fix: Route every world rebuild through ONE pair of functions — `clearSceneEntities()` (remove ALL derived meshes: enemies, villagers, lore; reset their arrays/sets and flags) and `spawnWorldEntities()` (recreate them for the new world). Archetype-switch, load, and initial setup all call the same pair.
- Why this fix: Derived state that mirrors a source must be torn down and rebuilt as a *unit* whenever the source is replaced. Partial, per-call-site cleanup inevitably forgets one collection (leak/duplicate) or one binding (stale-id desync). A single clear+respawn path makes "rebuild the world" mean rebuild *all* of it, by construction.
- Rule of thumb: When a source-of-truth object is replaced at runtime, tear down and recreate ALL derived/mirrored state through one shared clear+respawn path — never per-call-site partial cleanup, which leaks the collection you forgot and desyncs the bindings you didn't refresh.

## E4 — zero loop tail when repeat off
- Date: 2026-07-01
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Shadow
- Tags: [audio][state]
- What: A tone "sequence" type that can either loop or play once; each step is followed by a `loopDelay` gap before the next.
- Why built: The loop delay spaces repeats of a looping sequence.
- Design: After the final step, the same `loopDelay` was applied before deciding whether to repeat.
- Got right: Looping sequences spaced correctly.
- Where/why it failed: A NON-looping (one-shot) sequence stayed in the "playing" state for ~2 extra seconds because its final step still added the trailing `loopDelay` even though nothing repeats. It was invisible until one-shots played inline (on the same screen) instead of on a separate player screen, where the lingering "playing" state became obvious.
- Fix: Zero the tail delay when `repeat` is off — the last step of a one-shot has no gap after it.
- Why this fix: A between-repeats delay is only meaningful between repeats; when there is no next iteration, the trailing gap is dead time that keeps transient state alive. The state duration should equal the actual audible duration.
- Rule of thumb: A "between-items" delay must be dropped on the final item when there's no repeat — otherwise a one-shot lingers in a transient (playing/busy) state for the length of a gap that never precedes anything. Watch for it when the same code path serves both looping and one-shot modes.

## E1 — branch protection app bypass
- Date: 2026-07-02
- Project: pappydapimp69/memory
- Tags: [process][git][ci]
- What: Branch protection on `main` was set up expressly to force all canon
  changes through pull requests (so the `ENFORCE_INCOMING_ONLY` CI scope check
  fires), yet an agent session pushed straight to `main` immediately afterwards
  and the push landed.
- Why built: Close the write-bypass — `validate.yml` only enforces the
  contributors-touch-only-`incoming/` rule on `pull_request` events, so direct
  pushes skip the scope check entirely.
- Design: GitHub ruleset on the default branch with "require a pull request
  before merging" intended as the involuntary gate.
- Got right: The intent — moving enforcement from prose to a refusing gate is
  the correct fix for discipline failures.
- Where/why it failed: GitHub Apps (and anyone on the ruleset's bypass list)
  can be exempt from rulesets; an inactive/misconfigured ruleset also enforces
  nothing and gives no warning at push time. The gate *looked* closed and
  wasn't — a silent no-op, invisible until someone actually tested it.
- Fix: After configuring protection, TEST it with a real push from the actor
  you meant to constrain; check Enforcement = Active and audit the Bypass list
  (remove app/admin exemptions if strict PR-only is wanted).
- Why this fix: A protection rule is a claim, not a fact — only an attempted
  push distinguishes an active gate from a decorative one, and the failure mode
  is silent by design.
- Rule of thumb: Never trust an access-control rule you haven't watched refuse
  something — test the gate with the actor it's meant to stop.

## E9 — playwright port race and gesture audio
- Date: 2026-06-30
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [gamedev][harness][process]
- What: Verifying a browser game headlessly with Playwright — a full-slice end-to-end driver plus a screenshot script, run against a local `python3 -m http.server`.
- Design: A Playwright driver completes the full slice via `window.__game` hooks while a separate screenshot script runs back-to-back, each spawning its own `python3 -m http.server` on a fixed port (8099); audio via a WebAudio `AudioContext` constructed at module load.
- Why built: To prove a 3D mobile game actually plays (create→quests→combat→boss→victory) and to capture screenshots, all in CI/headless.
- Got right: Driving the game through exposed `window.__game` hooks (move/attack/interact/world-getter) made the whole slice scriptable and deterministic to test.
- Where/why it failed: Two distinct flakes. (1) Port race: a full-slice test and a screenshot script were launched back-to-back bound to the SAME port (8099); the second server (or a lingering first) contended, and the full-slice test failed mid-run ("boss did not spawn") even though the game was fine — a green re-run on a fresh port confirmed it. The failure looked like a game bug but was a harness collision. (2) Silent audio: WebAudio produced no sound and `AudioContext` stayed suspended because it was created at load, not on a user gesture — mobile/desktop autoplay policy blocks audio until a gesture resumes/creates the context.
- Fix: (1) Give every concurrent Playwright run its own port (or run strictly sequentially and wait for the server to exit); never share a localhost port between two drivers. (2) Create/resume the AudioContext inside the first user-gesture handler (button tap / "start" click), not at module load.
- Why this fix: Two processes binding/serving the same port is an unsynchronized resource conflict — classic flake that masquerades as a logic bug. And browsers deliberately keep audio suspended until a gesture, so any pre-gesture AudioContext is silent by design.
- Rule of thumb: One port per concurrent headless server (or serialize them) — a shared port turns into phantom "logic" failures on re-run. And always gesture-initialize WebAudio (create/resume in a click/tap handler), or audio is silently dead on mobile.

## E10 — a chronicle is not a story polish cannot fix structure
- Date: 2026-07-03
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [narrative][structure]
- What: A narrated short film meant to make an audience care about how the memory
  system works, built from real project events in chronological order.
- Why built: To win an audience vote against a data-visualization of the same events.
- Design: Real events, in order, with cute characters, a score, and narration layered
  on top — a chronological montage.
- Got right: The real-data grounding and an accessible framing for newcomers.
- Where/why it failed: Three polish passes (slower pacing, spoken narration,
  plain-language captions) and viewers still called it boring. An audit against basic
  story craft showed why: the protagonist was an OBJECT — born, filed, retrieved,
  placed — with no want, no opposition, no stakes, no choice. Chronological events
  with a theme are a chronicle; polish (pacing, voice, typography) cannot supply the
  missing engine.
- Fix: Structural rewrite, not polish — give the protagonist a want, escalating
  opposition, a moment of genuine choice under stakes, and a change; connect scenes
  with because/therefore/but, not and-then.
- Why this fix: Want/opposition/stakes/choice/change is the minimum engine that turns
  a sequence into a story; the same visuals and data can carry it once the meaning of
  the scenes changes.
- Rule of thumb: If feedback says a narrative artifact is boring, audit for
  want/opposition/stakes/choice BEFORE touching pacing, audio, or text — "and then"
  beats are a chronicle; "because/therefore/but" beats are a story. (Boundary: this
  applies when the goal is to move/engage; when the goal is to TEACH a mechanism to a
  novice, clarity can rightly outrank dramatic structure — that fork is a tension.)

## E11 — caption pinned timewarp keeps text and motion synced
- Date: 2026-07-03
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [animation][sync]
- What: A narrated canvas film whose scene animation and its captions must stay in
  lock-step, where caption durations are data-sized (they depend on text length).
- Why built: Viewer feedback said captions flew by and the animation drifted out of
  sync with the words.
- Design: First version related the two timelines with a single fixed scale factor
  per scene (real-time * k = animation clock), and sized each caption's on-screen
  window from its character count.
- Got right: Text-driven caption durations (a scene lasts as long as its words need)
  removed all hand-tuned scene lengths.
- Where/why it failed: A single scale factor broke synchronization. A long caption
  pushed later captions' windows far past their intended animation moments, so a
  line like "It jumped." appeared seconds after the character visibly jumped. Two
  independent clocks (text layout vs authored motion) had no shared anchor, and any
  caption edit silently re-broke sync.
- Fix: A per-scene piecewise-linear time warp. Define warp points [(0,0),
  (captionStart_i, animationAnchor_i)..., (sceneDur, base)]; the animation clock is
  warp(realTime), and sound beats are placed with the inverse warp. The captions
  become the pins the motion clock is stretched between.
- Why this fix: It makes the caption anchors the single source of truth for BOTH
  text and motion, so sync is correct by construction and editing a caption
  re-times the scene without touching choreography. Side benefit: motion slows
  under long captions, which reads as intentional pacing.
- Rule of thumb: When one timeline is data-sized (text/audio) and another is
  authored (animation), never relate them with a scale factor — pin them at
  semantic anchor points and piecewise-warp between the pins.

## E12 — narration timeline pace to speech not estimate
- Date: 2026-07-04
- Project: Pappydapimp69/Test
- Tags: [tts][voice]
- What: A narrated canvas film whose captions and scene beats must land in sync with
  a voice-over produced by the browser's Web Speech API.
- Why built: The film needed captions and animation to match the spoken narration on
  any device.
- Design: Each caption had a reading window computed from its text length; the film
  clock advanced at that estimated rate.
- Got right: The visual pacing itself, and sizing windows off the spoken line rather
  than the shortened on-screen line.
- Where/why it failed: The estimate drifted badly against real speech. Synthesized
  voices vary in rate per device/voice, and long lines ran seconds past their computed
  window — so the next caption and its narration began while the previous sentence was
  still being spoken, cutting the voice off. Reported repeatedly; pause/pacing tweaks
  never fixed it because the timeline and the voice were two independent clocks.
- Fix: Pace the timeline to the engine's actual speech state. In the frame loop, while
  speechSynthesis.speaking is true, hold the clock so it cannot advance past the next
  caption's start (or the scene boundary) until the current utterance ends — with a
  hard safety valve (~25s) so a stuck or absent voice cannot freeze playback. Reading
  estimates remain only as the fallback when narration is off/unsupported.
- Why this fix: The narrator is the authority on how long a line takes; ask it, do not
  guess. Once the clock yields to speech state, sync is correct on every device, and
  the forced inter-line pauses added to "buy time" for the estimate could be removed.
- Rule of thumb: When syncing visuals to text-to-speech, drive the timeline off the
  engine's real speaking state, not an estimated reading time — TTS duration varies per
  device/voice and any estimate eventually cuts the voice off.

  not coverage for animation.

  silently diverge; verify a time-based renderer with a full seek-sweep of the whole
  duration, because spot-check frames skip most of the code paths.

## E1 — cross platform cli windows papercuts
- Date: 2026-07-04
- Project: pappydapimp69/Brain
- Tags: [process][tooling][cross-platform]
- What: A Python stdlib CLI (`brain`) written and tested on Linux, then run for
  the first time by an agent (Codex) inside a Windows sandbox. It "worked" but
  the agent had to fight through three separate Windows-only breakages before
  the commands ran clean.
- Why built: The CLI is meant to be vendor- and OS-neutral — any agent with a
  shell should be able to install it, link a project, and query the system.
- Design: `install` symlinks the CLI onto PATH; a git `pre-commit` hook shells
  out to `python3`; command output uses status glyphs (`✓`, `✗`, `🔴`/`🟡`).
- Got right: The core logic (clone cache, link, pull node repos, read-only
  status) was genuinely portable and needed no change — the failures were all
  at the OS boundary, not in the logic.
- Where/why it failed: Three distinct Windows papercuts, each a silent or fatal
  stop for a Unix-shaped assumption. (1) `os.symlink` needs admin/developer mode
  on Windows, so `install` couldn't put `brain` on PATH. (2) The hook invoked
  `python3`, which on Windows is usually just `python`. (3) The default Windows
  console encoding (cp1252) cannot encode `✓` or emoji, so Python raised
  UnicodeEncodeError and crashed mid-print — the command had actually completed
  its work, but died displaying the success line.
- Fix: (1) On Windows write a `brain.cmd` shim into a cache `bin/` dir and append
  it to the *User* PATH via PowerShell, instead of a symlink. (2) Make the hook
  `py=$(command -v python3 || command -v python)`. (3) Detect UTF capability from
  the original stdout encoding, `reconfigure(errors="replace")` as a crash net,
  and swap glyphs for ASCII fallbacks (`[ok]`, `(open)`, `->`) when the console
  isn't UTF-capable.
- Why this fix: Each fix meets Windows where it is rather than assuming a POSIX
  shell — shim over symlink, python-or-python3, degrade-don't-die on encoding.
  The result is clean ASCII on a legacy console and pretty glyphs on a UTF one,
  never a crash.
- Rule of thumb: A stdlib CLI that "should be cross-platform" almost always has
  three Unix assumptions baked in — symlinks, the `python3` name, and unicode
  console output. Test on a real Windows console early; assume none of the three
  until you have.

- Rule of thumb: When onboarding an already-built RPG prototype, prefer a small state overlay that observes real systems over reshuffling global campaign indexes. Gate the first exit if needed, and map tutorial objects narrowly so they do not satisfy later quests.

## E1 — canonical hash prng state no transcendentals
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [gamedev][state][testing][determinism]
- What: Golden replay/fingerprint tests hashed authoritative state via
  `JSON.stringify`, whose key order is insertion-dependent (integer-like keys
  silently reorder first per the JS spec), so two logically-identical states
  could hash differently. Separately, following the `{seed,count}` seeded-RNG
  kernel literally meant restoring a save had to replay `count` rolls (O(n)
  on every load). And nothing guarded against transcendental `Math` calls
  (`sin`/`cos`/`pow`/…) inside the "deterministic" sim, even though those are
  engine-implementation-defined and can diverge across runtimes/browsers.
- Why built: Stage 0 of a from-scratch deterministic game harness
  (sim-before-render), implementing Brain's existing
  `[SYSTEM/testing/replay-fingerprint]` and
  `[SYSTEM/determinism/seeded-rng]` kernels for real.
- Design: (1) a canonical serializer that recursively sorts object keys and
  throws loud on NaN/Infinity/undefined/-0 before hashing (FNV-1a) for the
  golden test; (2) an sfc32 PRNG whose full 4-word integer state is saved
  directly inside the world state, no replay needed to restore; (3) a smoke-
  suite guard that greps `src/sim/*.js` for banned `Math.*` transcendental
  calls and fails the build if found — IEEE-exact ops (sqrt/floor/ceil/round/
  min/max/imul) stay allowed.
- Got right: the existing kernels (golden fingerprint test, seeded-RNG
  discipline) were correct in principle — this only sharpens the concrete
  implementation of each.
- Where/why it failed: a first pass hashed with plain `JSON.stringify` and
  failed a same-object-different-insertion-order unit test immediately; a
  naive `{seed,count}` port also required O(count) work on every load,
  caught by a save/load-mid-run test.
- Fix: implement `stableStringify` (sorted keys, reject non-finite/undefined,
  normalize -0); switch RNG persistence to raw integer state words instead of
  a replay count; add a grep-based transcendental-Math guard to the smoke
  suite so the invariant is mechanical, not a code-review convention.
- Why this fix: keeps the hash a pure function of logical state rather than
  incidental key-insertion order; makes save/load O(1) instead of O(n); and
  makes "no engine-varying math in the authoritative sim" something a build
  fails on, not something a reviewer has to remember to check.
- Rule of thumb: when hashing game state for a golden/regression test, always
  canonicalize (sorted keys, reject non-finite values) before hashing; save a
  PRNG's raw state words, never a replay count; and mechanically guard
  (grep/lint) against transcendental Math in any code claimed deterministic —
  don't rely on review to catch it.

## E2 — capture input edges at event time
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [input][gamedev][harness]
- What: input handling sampled logical key/touch state once per animation
  frame; a press-and-release shorter than one frame (a fast tap, or any
  synthetic/automated input) could land and lift entirely between two polls
  and be silently dropped.
- Why built: Stage 2's unified input module (keyboard/touch/gamepad -> one
  command vocabulary), exercised end-to-end with Playwright.
- Design: originally `poll()` read `held`/`touches` state fresh each frame
  and derived press-edges purely from that per-frame snapshot.
- Got right: per-frame sampling is simple and correct for anything held
  across multiple frames (movement, gamepad sticks).
- Where/why it failed: Playwright's `keyboard.press()` fires a keydown+keyup
  pair essentially instantly; the e2e suite went from green to 4/4 failing
  the moment this was exercised, because the resulting quest-accept/attack
  commands never reached the sim's dispatch — the press-and-release
  happened between two polls.
- Fix: capture presses at EVENT time (keydown/touchstart/mousedown) into a
  `pending` queue; the next `poll()` call consumes that queue into the
  frame's `down` state regardless of whether the key/touch is still
  physically down by the time polling happens. Continuously-held state
  (movement) still comes from `held`/`touches`, sampled per frame as before.
- Why this fix: separates "was this pressed at all since the last poll"
  (event-driven, cannot be missed) from "is this currently held"
  (state-driven, fine to sample) — the bug was conflating the two.
- Rule of thumb: any input system polled once per frame must capture
  discrete press events at event time into a queue, not derive them purely
  from per-frame state sampling — a press/release pair faster than one frame
  is invisible to pure sampling, and this affects real fast human input, not
  just automated tests.
- Provenance (verified/assumed): verified
- Link: [redacted]

## E3 — device hint computed at render time
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [input][ui][gamedev]
- What: modal/dialog buttons showed a baked-in key hint ("Accept (Enter)",
  "Later (Esc)") regardless of which input device was actually active. A
  gamepad player saw "(Enter)"/"(Esc)" prompts even though A/B were the
  buttons that actually worked (the underlying action ids were already
  device-agnostic, so input functionally worked — only the printed text was
  wrong).
- Why built: each modal's button objects were constructed with a fully
  baked label string (`label: 'Accept (Enter)'`) at the moment the modal
  was opened, based on whatever assumption was convenient at that call site.
- Design: the modal-button id vocabulary (`confirm`/`cancel`/`alt`) was
  already device-agnostic by construction (it doubles as the unified input
  layer's action names) — the bug was purely in the STRING shown to the
  player, not in which input actually triggered the button.
- Got right: this project already had the underlying lesson applied at one
  layer — `pappydapimp69/test#E3` established labeling on-screen (touch)
  buttons with words instead of glyphs, and polling the gamepad every frame.
  That lesson covers *which glyphs/words a persistent on-screen button
  shows*; it doesn't cover *dialog/modal hint text that's baked in once at
  creation time*.
- Where/why it failed: found visually — a modal opened while a gamepad was
  the active device still displayed keyboard-specific hint text, an easy
  bug to miss because the buttons still worked (A confirmed, B canceled);
  only the displayed text was stale/wrong, so nothing crashed or looked
  broken in an automated functional test.
- Fix: strip the baked suffix from every button's base label; compute the
  device-specific hint fresh every render frame from the currently active
  device, via a small `keyHint(device, action) -> string` lookup
  (keyboard/gamepad/touch, where touch returns '' since the on-screen button
  IS the input and needs no key hint), and append it at draw time.
- Why this fix: a modal can stay open across a device switch (e.g. the
  player picks up a controller mid-dialog), and even when it doesn't, the
  device that was active *when the modal object was constructed* is not
  guaranteed to be the device active when it's rendered — computing the
  hint at render time removes the staleness class entirely rather than
  chasing each call site that got the assumption wrong.
- Rule of thumb: any UI text that names a physical input control (a key, a
  button glyph) must be derived from the CURRENTLY active device at the
  moment of rendering, never baked in at the moment the UI element was
  constructed — treat "which device is active" as frame-state to read, not
  as a fact to snapshot once.

## E4 — duplicated text logic drifts fix on first sight
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [process][gamedev]
- What: the same small piece of presentation logic — turning a quest
  objective's data (`{type, target/item/zone, n}`) into player-facing prose
  — was independently written in two UI surfaces: an in-world HUD quest
  tracker, and a quest-offer dialog. When a third objective type (`reach`)
  was added to the data model, one call site was updated and the other
  wasn't, producing a visible bug ("Find the undefined") in the
  never-updated site. This was the SECOND time the same underlying mistake
  happened — the first duplicate had already drifted once, independently,
  earlier in the project, and was fixed in only that one location without
  anyone noticing the sibling copy existed.
- Why built: each UI surface was written at a different point in the build
  (a HUD tracker in an earlier stage, an offer dialog in a later one), each
  time reimplementing the same small switch/ternary rather than reusing
  existing logic, because the duplication wasn't obvious in the moment —
  the two call sites are visually and structurally unrelated (a corner HUD
  readout vs. a modal dialog).
- Design: no shared function existed; each site had its own inline
  `type === 'kill' ? ... : ...` mapping.
- Got right: nothing structural — this is a pure process/discipline lesson,
  not a design tradeoff.
- Where/why it failed: adding a new objective TYPE is exactly the kind of
  change the project's own content/code seam is supposed to make cheap and
  safe (`SYSTEM/content/objective-types` in ideas) — but that seam only
  covers the REDUCER's handling of the type, not incidental presentation
  logic that also happens to branch on type. The seam has a blind spot at
  its edges: anything else in the codebase that also switches on the same
  discriminant needs to be found and updated too, and there was no single
  place enumerating "everywhere that branches on objective type."
- Fix: extract the duplicated logic into one function
  (`describeObjective(objective)`) used by both call sites, immediately
  upon noticing the duplication — not deferred until it caused a second bug.
- Why this fix: a single source of truth for "how does this data become
  text" makes the next new type addition a one-place edit instead of a
  find-every-caller exercise; the fix is boring but the alternative (finding
  every duplicate by hand, after each new discriminant value) doesn't scale
  and had already failed once.
- Rule of thumb: the moment you notice the same small transformation
  (especially "turn this data into player-facing text") written in two
  places, extract it right then — don't wait for it to drift a second time.
  A code/content seam that makes adding a new TYPE cheap in the reducer
  doesn't automatically protect every OTHER place in the codebase that also
  branches on that type; those call sites need their own single source of
  truth, or they will silently miss new cases one at a time.

## E5 — husk quest tutorial text vs mechanic confusion
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [gamedev][gamedesign][ux]
- What: A player reported that killing quest-target enemies (husks) with the
  basic melee attack didn't advance a "Defeat 2 husks" kill-count objective,
  but killing them with the aura-blast special attack did — implying the
  quest was silently weapon-gated, with the game having only 2 husks total
  and no respawn, so a melee-only player would look permanently stuck at 0/2.
- Why built: Investigated to find and fix the described weapon-gating bug.
- Design: The kill-tracking code (`hitEnemy` → `questProgress` in
  `src/sim/reduce.js`) passes the DEFEATED ENEMY's kind to the quest
  tracker, never the attack kind — so on a straight code read it already
  looked weapon-agnostic, contradicting the report.
- Got right: A headless ground-truth test (`makeWorld` → `TALK` → `ACCEPT_QUEST`
  → `MELEE` a husk to death → check `quests.active[...].progress`) proved
  melee kills DID advance the objective identically to blast kills, and a
  full melee-only playthrough completed the whole quest. So there was no
  weapon-gating bug in the mechanic at all — code reading gave the same
  conclusion the test confirmed, for once.
- Where/why it failed: The REAL source of the confusion was adjacent tutorial
  text — a separate onboarding checklist (`arc.steps`, gating when the
  end-game boss can spawn) had two teaching-step guide lines shown one at a
  time: "A husk blocks the road. Strike it down up close." (melee step),
  immediately followed once satisfied by "Charge your aura, then blast a
  husk from range." (aura step). The aura line names "a husk" specifically,
  even though that teaching step actually only requires ONE aura hit landed
  on ANY living enemy (a different always-present enemy works fine) — it has
  nothing to do with the quest's kill count. A player who kills a husk with
  melee, then immediately sees "blast a husk from range" surface in the same
  onscreen guide slot the quest tracker lives next to, reasonably reads it as
  "the game is telling me this kill needs to be an aura kill" — a plausible
  misreading with real consequences (the only 2 quest-targets are limited
  and non-respawning), even though the state was correct the whole time.
- Fix: (1) Reworded the aura guide line to "Charge your aura, then loose a
  blast at any foe from range." — removing the enemy-specific noun that
  implied a link to the quest's specific kill targets. (2) The stall-risk
  ask ("husks need to respawn so the quest can never permanently stall")
  went through two design iterations in the same session: a first pass
  added a runtime respawn-timer safety net (kill objective short + all
  unlocked targets of that kind dead -> a fresh copy reappears after a
  tick-gated delay); the human then redirected to a different, better
  design — instead of respawn insurance, the quest's two husks became two
  DISTINCT VARIANTS (`ironhusk`, immune to melee; `wardhusk`, immune to
  aura), each killable by exactly one method, so the quest is unstallable
  BY CONSTRUCTION (the other method always works on the other husk) rather
  than by a timer catching a hypothetical failure after the fact. A new
  generic `immune` field on the enemyKind schema (reusable for future
  enemies/buffs) plus a distinct `no_effect` refusal event (never a silent
  no-op on a wrong-method hit) shipped instead of the respawn timer, which
  was fully removed. Splitting the single "kill 2 husks" objective into two
  specific-kind objectives was a second, independent benefit of the same
  change: the quest tracker now shows two differentiated lines instead of
  one generic counter.
- Why this fix: Text that happens to name the same noun ("husk") as a
  quest's target, shown in a UI region physically next to a HUD element
  tracking that noun's kill count, will be read as if the two are the same
  requirement — even when the two subsystems (a one-time onboarding
  checklist vs. a quest's kill objective) are completely unrelated in code.
  Wording that stays generic ("any foe") when a mechanic doesn't actually
  care about a specific target avoids implying a false requirement.
- Rule of thumb: When two independent systems both react to the same kind of
  game event (here: hitting/killing an enemy of a named kind) and their UI
  surfaces render near each other, audit whether either system's PLAYER-
  FACING TEXT accidentally implies a dependency between them that the CODE
  doesn't have — a player will treat adjacent, similarly-worded on-screen
  text as one instruction, regardless of how separate the underlying state
  machines are. Prove root cause with a ground-truth headless test before
  patching perceived logic bugs — the code and the UX confusion here had two
  entirely different, unrelated locations.

## E6 — input press vocabulary not fixed list
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [input][gamedev][ui]
- What: the unified input module's edge-triggered "presses" output was
  computed only for a fixed, hardcoded list of action names; any UI zone with
  a dynamically-chosen id outside that list (a title screen's "New Game" /
  "Controls" / archetype-card buttons) never produced a press event, even
  though it was captured into the same event-time `pending` queue as
  everything else.
- Why built: Stage 5 added a title screen (Continue/New Game/Controls,
  archetype + difficulty picker) reusing the same click/tap-zone hit-testing
  already used for in-game modals.
- Design: modal buttons had always used ids like `confirm`/`cancel`/`alt` —
  which happen to already be members of the fixed action vocabulary — so
  they worked by construction, which masked the limitation until a UI with
  differently-named zones was built.
- Got right: reusing one zone-hit-testing mechanism for both modals and a
  full title screen instead of building a second UI input path.
- Where/why it failed: the first test of the title screen found clicks/taps
  did nothing; traced to `poll()` only promoting names in a fixed `ACTIONS`
  array into the returned `presses` object, silently dropping any other
  captured id.
- Fix: any id captured into the event-time `pending` queue this frame — not
  just ids in the fixed action vocabulary — is now promoted into `presses`
  as a one-shot press. The fixed-list edge-detection (comparing against
  previous-frame state) is kept only for the small set of names that can
  also be continuously HELD (keyboard/gamepad), since those need real
  edge-detection to avoid re-firing every frame; one-shot event-captured ids
  need no such filtering.
- Why this fix: the original design conflated "the small vocabulary of
  continuously-holdable actions" with "the general set of anything a UI zone
  can be tapped or clicked," which only coincidentally lined up for modals.
- Rule of thumb: when a unified input module exposes a one-shot "press"
  concept for UI hit-zones, don't gate it behind the same fixed vocabulary
  used for held/repeatable actions — any dynamically-labeled clickable or
  tappable zone must be able to fire a press under its own id.
