## E7 — press and hold charge percent shaped rate
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [input][gamedesign]
- What: a "charge aura" meter action was implemented as tap-to-add-a-flat-
  amount, edge-triggered off a single button press. This rewards mashing the
  button as fast as possible over holding it down, which reads wrong for a
  hold-to-charge fantasy and doesn't match how the mechanic is described to
  the player.
- Why built: charge was originally just another one-shot command like
  melee/interact, dispatched once per button-press edge.
- Design (the fix): press-and-hold via an authoritative hold-counter
  (`player.chargeHold`) that resets to 0 on a fresh press (an explicit
  `start: true` flag on the command) and increments on every subsequent tick
  while held; gain-per-tick ramps mildly with consecutive ticks held. The
  rate is ALSO reshaped by the meter's CURRENT fill percent: boosted when
  charging from empty, and hard-throttled — ignoring the ramp bonus entirely
  — once at or above ~80% full, so topping off the last stretch takes real
  hold time no matter how "warmed up" the hold's ramp is.
- Got right: the input layer already had a fixed one-shot action vocabulary
  (button-press edges); extending it to also expose continuous *held* state
  (already computed internally for movement/gamepad polling, just not
  surfaced) was a small, additive change, not a rearchitecture.
- Where/why it failed: player feedback specifically — the old scheme let a
  fast tapper fill the meter as effectively as (or faster than) someone
  holding the button, defeating the intended "commit to holding" feel.
- Fix: `CHARGE` becomes a repeated command dispatched on a fixed real-time
  cadence for as long as the button is held (not once per animation frame,
  to stay frame-rate independent-ish); the sim computes gain from
  `(holdTicks, currentPercent)` using only integer arithmetic (`Math.floor`
  ratios) to keep the authoritative state's determinism invariant intact —
  no floats, no engine-varying math.
- Why this fix: the ramp+percent shaping is entirely inside the
  deterministic reducer (testable with exact expected sequences), while only
  the "is the button still down" signal and the fixed dispatch cadence live
  in the presentation layer — the sim doesn't need to know about wall-clock
  time, only about how many hold-ticks have occurred.
- Rule of thumb: when a meter-fill mechanic is meant to feel like "commit to
  holding," (a) drive it from continuous held state, not edge-triggered taps
  — expose held state from the input layer even if only edge-triggered
  presses existed before; (b) reset any hold-duration ramp explicitly on a
  fresh press so a released-and-repressed hold doesn't inherit stale
  momentum; (c) shape the rate by the meter's CURRENT value, not just hold
  duration, if the design wants "fast from empty, slow near full" — and make
  the near-full throttle override the ramp bonus entirely, or a long enough
  hold defeats the intended slowdown.

- Link: [redacted]

## E8 — prologue entry only cache bust tradeoff
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [browser][tooling][process]
- What: A player reported a just-shipped press-and-hold charge fix "still not working" on both keyboard and gamepad, even though CI confirmed the deploy succeeded. Re-verification (fresh Playwright repro against the local repo, a new mocked-gamepad sustained-hold e2e, press/release/repress cycles, and a direct tick-by-tick simulation of the 80%+ throttle) found the mechanic was already correct — no code bug.
- Why built: Build-step-free static ES modules on GitHub Pages, same shape as the sibling Dog Park 3D project (projects/pappydapimp69__dog.md#E5): `index.html` loads `<script type="module" src="src/main.js">` with no version token, and `main.js` statically imports ~15 other modules (game.js, input.js, reduce.js, ...) each by plain relative URL.
- Design: Cache-busting was applied ONLY to the entry script (`src/main.js?v=<manual date+counter>`), not propagated into the ~30 relative import specifiers across the module graph.
- Got right: Confirmed via a fresh, independent repro that the underlying press-and-hold logic (keyboard AND mocked-gamepad sustained hold, hold/release/re-hold ramp reset, 80%+ throttle never producing 2+ consecutive zero-gain ticks) was correct all along — didn't waste more effort chasing a phantom logic bug once caching was identified as plausible.
- Where/why it failed: An entry-point-only cache-bust guarantees a fresh fetch of `main.js` itself, but each statically-imported submodule is a SEPARATE cache entry keyed by its own unversioned URL — bumping the entry script's query string does nothing for those. A browser/tab that loaded the game before the fix shipped can still be running stale `game.js`/`input.js` from its own cache (or from an already-open, never-reloaded tab) even after the entry script is refetched.
- Fix: Applied the cheap partial fix explicitly in scope (entry-only `?v=` bump on `index.html`'s script tag, hand-maintained since there's no build step to compute a commit SHA) and documented — rather than silently leaving it implied — that this does NOT fully solve staleness for the nested import graph. Full-graph busting (matching the Dog project's actual fix: a deploy-time `sed` step rewriting every relative import specifier with a per-deploy token) was identified as the complete solution but deferred as out of scope for this fix.
- Why this fix: Matches the task's explicit scope (a cheap defensive mitigation, not a guaranteed full solution) while being honest in the commit message about the residual gap, so a future "still broken after deploy" report isn't re-diagnosed as a logic bug again without first checking whether THIS TIME it's the nested-import gap rather than the entry point.
- Rule of thumb: For build-step-free static ES-module apps, an entry-point-only `?v=` cache-bust is necessary but not sufficient — it does not cache-bust modules reached via nested relative imports, which are independent cache entries. Either bust every import specifier at deploy time (sed/build-token substitution, as in the Dog project) or explicitly document the partial-coverage tradeoff so it isn't mistaken for a complete fix.
- Provenance (verified/assumed): verified
- Link: [redacted]

## E9 — quest gated entities agnostic of history
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: Pappydapimp69/The-game-prologue
- Tags: [gamedev][quest][state]
- What: quest objectives only counted progress from events that happened
  while the quest was already `active`. Killing the two husks or grabbing
  the training capsule *before* talking to the quest-giver and accepting
  permanently soft-locked "Clear the Road" at 0/2 — nothing respawned, and
  there was no path back to completion.
- Why built: a standard kill/collect/reach quest system layered over an
  open-world region where the relevant enemies and pickup were already
  present in the world from game start, for exploration/combat purposes
  independent of the quest.
- Design: `questProgress()` iterated `state.quests.active` and incremented
  progress on matching events; nothing tracked what had happened to those
  entities *before* acceptance.
- Got right: quests were already "offered, never pushed" (accepting requires
  an explicit command) — the surrounding design was sound, this was a
  narrower state-tracking gap.
- Where/why it failed: live playtesting (a human, not an automated test)
  killed the husks and grabbed the capsule while exploring before ever
  talking to the NPC, then found the quest permanently stuck.
- Fix: rejected retroactively crediting progress from world state at the
  moment of acceptance (still history-dependent, just delayed, and easy to
  get subtly wrong — e.g. double-counting a kill both retroactively and via
  a future duplicate event). Instead, the objective's target entities don't
  exist in the world AT ALL until the quest is accepted — `ACCEPT_QUEST`
  spawns them from a `quest.unlocks` list declared in content, built once at
  world-construction time and instantiated on accept. There is no "already
  did it" case to special-case because nothing was there to act on yet.
- Why this fix: objectives become provably agnostic of anything that
  happened before acceptance, by construction rather than by careful
  bookkeeping — a stronger and simpler invariant than retroactive credit.
  A non-gated entity (an always-present enemy, in this case) remains
  available for open exploration and as a stable target for mechanic-only
  tests that don't care about quest state.
- Rule of thumb: if a quest objective's completability could depend on
  actions taken before the quest exists to the player, don't try to detect
  and credit those actions after the fact — make the objective's target
  entities not exist until the quest is accepted. This is the mirror image
  of `local__dbh.md#E5` ("map tutorial objects narrowly so EARLY actions
  don't satisfy LATER quests") — same terrain (order-dependent quest state),
  opposite direction (here, early actions couldn't satisfy quest objectives
  *at all* once the quest existed).

## E1 — wrongsky restore canvas transform in blit
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-wrong-sky
- Tags: [gamedev][rendering][canvas]
- What: A render helper that changes the canvas transform to blit a full-screen layer must restore the prior transform, or the caller's next world-space draws land in raw screen space.
- Why built: A 2D game restores a "light" facet by compositing an offscreen darkness-with-light-holes layer over the world. The helper `drawLight()` did `ctx.setTransform(1,0,0,1,0,0)` so it could `drawImage` the layer in screen space, then returned. The caller — still "inside" the world transform conceptually — immediately redrew the wall occluders with `drawWall(x,y)` at world-tile coordinates (`x*TILE, y*TILE`).
- Design: The world is drawn under a camera transform set via `ctx.setTransform(scale,0,0,scale,-camX*scale,-camY*scale)`; the HUD is drawn later under an identity transform. Any helper that touches the transform sits between those two phases.
- Got right: The offscreen light layer + destination-out light holes composited cleanly; the bug was purely that the transform wasn't handed back.
- Where/why it failed: Caught by an adversarial code-review pass before ship (not by a test — canvas transform state isn't asserted by the headless smoke suite). Concretely: once a player attuned the light well (a mandatory quest step), the ridge walls redrew at raw pixel coordinates fixed in the upper-left of the viewport and no longer tracked the camera, while the true wall locations fell dark. Every session that restored light hit it.
- Fix: Wrap the transform change in `ctx.save()` / `ctx.restore()` around the screen-space blit inside the helper, so the caller's world transform is intact on return. (Equivalently: re-apply the world transform before the wall loop.)
- Why this fix: save/restore is local to the helper and self-documenting — the helper owns its transform mutation and cleans it up, rather than the caller having to know the helper left the transform dirty and re-establish it. Re-applying at the call site works but leaks the helper's implementation detail into the caller.
- Rule of thumb: Any drawing helper that calls `setTransform`/`translate`/`scale` on a shared canvas context must bracket it with `save()`/`restore()`. Canvas transform is global mutable state with no compile-time scope; a helper that leaves it changed is the 2D-graphics twin of a function that leaves a global variable dirty. Headless smoke tests won't catch it — the pixels are only wrong on screen — so it needs a render-path review or a screenshot diff.

## E2 — wrongsky snapshot run start for respawn
- Date: 2026-07-07
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-wrong-sky
- Tags: [gamedev][state][save]
- What: To restart a run on player death, snapshot the actual starting world state — do NOT rebuild it from the caller's construction options, because a resumed/continued game reaches the same restart path with those options empty.
- Why built: `startGame(canvas, seed, options, initialWorld)` builds a world from `options` (archetype/difficulty/imported-carryover) for a New Game, OR takes a loaded `initialWorld` for Continue. The in-game "Rise Again" (defeat) handler restarted the run with `world = makeWorld(seed, options)`.
- Design: A Continue calls `startGame(canvas, bootSeed, {}, loadedWorld)` — empty options, because the character lives in the loaded world, not in options. New Game passes the real options.
- Got right: The world itself carries everything needed (`world.settings.archetype/difficulty`, `world.seed`, current skills, RNG state) — the save round-trips it all. So the data to restart correctly was present; it was just being ignored in favor of the (empty) options.
- Where/why it failed: Found by an adversarial playability review before ship. Repro: Continue a saved Channeler/Seeker/Harsh/carryover run → die → "Rise Again" → you respawn as a default Gentle Brawler on the wrong seed, because `makeWorld(seed, {})` falls back to `defaultArchetype`/`'gentle'`/no-carryover. New-game runs were unaffected (their options were populated), which is exactly why casual testing missed it.
- Fix: At `startGame` entry, capture `const respawn = JSON.stringify(world)` (the run's true starting state, whether freshly built or loaded). On death, `world = JSON.parse(respawn)`. Also: never persist a defeated/ended world (`if (!world.flags.ended && world.player.hp > 0) saveGame(world)`), so a tab closed on the defeat screen doesn't resume a "zombie" at 0 HP.
- Why this fix: Snapshot-and-restore reproduces the exact start (archetype, difficulty, seed, carried skills, RNG) with zero dependence on which construction path was taken. Reconstructing from options only works for the path that happens to carry them; the snapshot works for all paths by construction.
- Rule of thumb: When "restart/respawn" and "initial construction" are two code paths that must produce the same state, don't re-run the constructor with its inputs on restart — snapshot the constructed state once and restore it. The inputs may be absent on the path that loaded (rather than built) the state, and the constructor will silently substitute defaults. Corollary: never autosave a terminal/defeated state over a good one, or a mid-death resume loads it.

## E1 — waitingcity full session respawn rules out attempt based design
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [gamedev][design][state]
- What: Before designing any mechanic that assumes a player will make several cheap attempts at the same encounter (attempt-based difficulty scaling, "learn from your losses" progression, retry-until-you-win loops), check what the game's actual respawn/retry mechanic reverts — not just HP, but session scope.
- Why built: Considering whether the new confidence-gated perception system (regular guards read hp/power/AI-state once the player has enough real encounters with that kind, win or loss — see the companion entry on validating the exception) should also apply to the one-shot Warden boss fight, via some attempt-counting scheme (e.g. "the Warden gets more readable each time you fight it").
- Design: Read `game.js`'s actual respawn implementation rather than assuming "die → retry the fight" is what happens.
- Got right: Didn't assume; went and read the code path that death actually triggers before designing around it.
- Where/why it failed: `const respawn = JSON.stringify(world)` is captured once at session start (see the related, already-filed lesson on why that snapshot exists — projects/pappydapimp69__the-game-wrong-sky.md, wrongsky-snapshot-run-start-for-respawn). On player death, `world = JSON.parse(respawn)` restores that ONE snapshot — reverting the entire session's progress (every quest, every kill, every item), not just the current fight. An attempt-based boss mechanic assumes "try, lose, try again" is cheap; here, losing to the boss costs the whole playthrough. The two designs are incompatible unless the respawn model itself changes.
- Fix: Kept the Warden on its original flat perception-stat gate instead — a deliberate, named exception to the new confidence system, not an oversight. No code needed changing for the boss at all; the fix was catching the mismatched assumption before writing code that would have been quietly unusable (an attempt-based reveal nobody could actually exercise more than once per session).
- Why this fix: Cheaper to discover "this mechanic has no reachable design space" by reading the retry path than to build the mechanic and find out via playtest that it never gets used because losing the boss fight means abandoning the run.
- Rule of thumb: A mechanic that scales with "number of attempts at X" is only viable if attempting X again is actually cheap for the player. Read the literal retry/respawn/reload code path for X before designing around repeated attempts — "you can always just try again" is a UX assumption, not a given, and a full-session-revert respawn (common in small deterministic-sim games that snapshot session start for simplicity) silently invalidates it for any one-shot encounter.

## E2 — waitingcity live ai breaks fixed coordinate e2e scripts
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [gamedev][harness][testing][determinism]
- What: A browser end-to-end test that drives a scripted, fixed-coordinate command sequence (walk to tile X,Y, attack entity at its assumed spawn position) against the LIVE running game — not a pure Node replay — silently breaks the moment the game has any enemy/NPC that moves on its own over real time, because real wall-clock time (menu navigation, screenshots, `waitForTimeout`) elapses before the scripted commands even start, during which the live render loop's own periodic tick already lets that entity wander.
- Why built: This game (the third in a saga) is the first installment where enemies have real AI — a per-kind state machine (patrol/chase/attack/return/flee) whose movement is decided once per `TICK` command, integer-BFS-pathed toward the player when aggroed. The prior two games' enemies never moved at all, so their existing browser e2e test (reuse the Node smoke suite's exact scripted `demoCommands()`, replayed via `window.__game.dispatch(cmd)` against the live game) worked fine — nothing in their worlds changed on its own between commands.
- Design: The Node-side golden-fingerprint regression test (`scripts/smoke.mjs`) replays `demoCommands()` through a bare `reduce()` loop with NO real time involved — deterministic by construction, and it passed cleanly (43/43). The browser e2e test reused the exact same `demoCommands()` array but dispatched each command through `window.__game.dispatch`, the live game's own wrapper, while the page's `requestAnimationFrame` loop kept running in the background.
- Got right: The Node-only pure-`reduce()` replay (no live loop involved) matched the golden fingerprint immediately and remains the correct, exact regression check for the deterministic sim — that part of the test design was sound and needed no change.
- Where/why it failed: `ACCEPT_QUEST: watch-them-move not offered` — a quest that should have already had its prerequisite satisfied, wasn't. Root cause, found by isolating a minimal repro: a scripted `MELEE` sequence assumed an enemy was still standing at its home tile, but the live game's own tick cadence (which starts firing the instant the intro modal is dismissed, since `nextTickAt` starts at 0) had already let that enemy patrol away by 1-2 tiles during the real milliseconds spent on menu navigation and screenshots — enough to put it outside melee range, so every scripted attack silently whiffed (`too_far`, a safe non-throwing event) and the quest's kill objective never completed. This is NOT a game bug — the enemy wandering is the AI system working exactly as designed; it's a mismatch between the test's assumption (static enemy positions, true only for the earlier games) and the new reality (this game's world changes on its own).
- Fix: Split the e2e test in two: (1) the pure-`reduce()`/`replay()` fingerprint check, unaffected by real time, kept exactly as before; (2) a separate, shorter live-game playthrough that reads each target entity's CURRENT position fresh from `window.__game.world()` before every step and walks/attacks toward wherever it actually is right now — i.e., the same thing a real player has to do, rather than a memorized coordinate.
- Why this fix: A live, ticking game's state is not a fixed script's input — any e2e harness driving the REAL running instance (as opposed to a pure sim replay) must treat entity positions as read-at-use-time, exactly as a human player or the production input layer does, once anything in the world can act on its own between the tester's steps.
- Rule of thumb: The moment a game gains any entity that moves/decides without direct player input (patrol AI, ambient traffic, scheduled spawns), any e2e test that drives the LIVE build (not a pure state-replay) must stop assuming fixed coordinates for that entity and instead re-read its position live before each interaction — a fixed-coordinate script is only safe against a world that provably never changes on its own, and adding real-time agency to the game silently invalidates that assumption in existing test harnesses.

## E3 — waitingcity shop npc must not also be a quest giver
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [gamedev][ui][state]
- What: Giving one NPC BOTH a shop and quest-giving duties, in a reducer/event architecture where a single `TALK` command can emit both a `'talked'` event (opened as a shop modal if the NPC has a shop) and a `'quests_offered'` event (which unconditionally overwrites the modal) in the same dispatch, makes the shop modal unreachable whenever a quest happens to be offerable — the quest-offer handler always wins the race, silently, with no error.
- Why built: Content for this game's opening added a second quest-giving NPC (a trainer, "Ossa") who ALSO runs a small shop, on the assumption that combining roles would read as more efficient content — one NPC instead of two separate stops.
- Design: `reduce()`'s `TALK` case always emits `'talked'` first, then appends `'quests_offered'` afterward if the NPC has any currently-offerable quest (prereqs met, not already active/completed). The presentation layer's event handler processes events in order: the `'talked'` handler opens a shop modal when `npc.shop` is truthy; the `'quests_offered'` handler unconditionally does `view.modal = mkModal('offer', ...)` with no guard for an already-open modal. This pattern was carried forward unchanged from the two earlier games in this saga, where it was never a problem because their one quest-giving NPC and their one shop NPC were always different characters.
- Got right: The underlying event/modal architecture itself is fine and doesn't need changing — the bug is purely in the CONTENT decision (one NPC given two roles that the presentation layer's dispatch order doesn't disambiguate), not in the reducer or renderer.
- Where/why it failed: Caught before ever running, by cross-referencing the new content draft against the established, previously-proven pattern (two prior games, both of which kept shop-NPCs and quest-giving-NPCs strictly disjoint) while writing this game's presentation-layer event handling — recognized that `game.js`'s `'quests_offered'` handler has no `if (!view.modal)` guard, unlike some of its sibling cases, so it would silently clobber whatever the `'talked'` handler had just set.
- Fix: Routed every quest in this game's content through the single dedicated quest-giver NPC; the shop NPC carries flavor dialog only, no `giver` role on any quest. (The npc-level `offers` field some content had used for readability turned out to be non-functional documentation only — the ACTUAL offer eligibility is driven entirely by each quest's own `giver` field, cross-referenced against `requires`/active/completed — so the fix was a pure content change, zero reducer/renderer code touched.)
- Why this fix: Cheaper and safer than teaching the event handlers to arbitrate a shop-vs-quest-offer race (e.g., queuing, priority flags, or a merged modal kind) for a scenario the content doesn't actually need — one NPC per role keeps the existing, already-battle-tested event ordering correct by construction, exactly like the two prior games did.
- Rule of thumb: In any event-sourced UI where a single command can emit multiple events that each independently set the SAME piece of view state (here: one modal reference) with no coordination between their handlers, never give one entity two roles whose event handlers both react to the same triggering command — audit for this specifically whenever content reuses an NPC/entity id across roles that used to be safely separate, rather than assuming the underlying dispatch code will arbitrate correctly just because each individual handler looks correct in isolation.

## E4 — waitingcity spawn telegraph must be sim side not cosmetic
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [gamedev][state][gamedesign]
- What: A "the enemy shouldn't just appear instantly" pacing complaint is a request about ATTACKABILITY (can it act on/be acted on yet), not sprite appearance — a presentation-only fade-in leaves the entity fully alive and able to strike the player the instant it's queued, so it doesn't actually address the complaint. The correct fix is a genuine sim-side delay: the entity does not exist in authoritative state at all until a scheduled tick arrives.
- Why built: A playtester reported "enemies shouldn't spawn instantly" against a quest-unlocked enemy that materialized fully formed in `state.enemies` (alive, full hp, immediately live for AI/collision) the instant `ACCEPT_QUEST` was dispatched — the same command that closed the offer dialog. Nothing was mechanically wrong (the existing existence-gating anti-softlock pattern, `projects/pappydapimp69__the-game-prologue.md#E9`, was working correctly) — the complaint was about *pacing/telegraphing*, not correctness.
- Design: Considered two approaches before building: (a) keep the instant sim-side spawn, add a renderer-only opacity ease-in on the sprite; (b) make the entity genuinely absent from `state.enemies` for a fixed window after the triggering command, only materializing on a later `TICK`. Went with (b).
- Got right: The existing sibling pattern in this same codebase — a boss that "appears" (dialog shown) and later "taunts" (a separate event fired later, gated on a state condition checked every `reduce()` call, not a wall-clock timer) — was the right precedent to generalize from: staging a reveal across ticks/conditions, never wall-clock time, keeps it inside the deterministic command-replay contract.
- Where/why it failed: N/A — caught and designed around before implementation, via review of the actual dispatch/render-gating code, not via a live bug. The rejected option (a) would have failed silently: the entity would already be a live AI actor and a valid `ENEMY_STRIKE`/`MELEE` target during its own "fade," so a player could be hit by (or hit) something still visually easing in — decoupling the mechanical timeline from the visual one is worse than an honest instant pop, because now the visual cue actively lies about what the sim guarantees.
- Fix: Added `state.pendingSpawns: [{id, tmpl, readyTick}]`. The triggering command (`ACCEPT_QUEST`) pushes an entry and emits an "incoming" event (for a toast/audio telegraph) instead of writing the entity into `state.enemies`; the per-tick reducer case drains any entry whose `readyTick` has arrived — BEFORE that tick's AI decisions run, so a freshly-materialized entity gets one free beat before it can act — and only then emits the "appeared" event and adds it to the real entity map. The delay is a small FIXED tick count, not drawn from the seeded RNG: the goal is predictable telegraphing, not unpredictability, and jitter would only add edge cases to replay/determinism tests for zero player-facing benefit.
- Why this fix: It's the only version where the visual telegraph and the mechanical truth agree — during the delay window the entity provably cannot strike or be struck, because it does not exist yet, the same guarantee `hasOwnProperty`-style existence-gating already relies on elsewhere in this codebase for a different purpose (soft-lock prevention). Two different problems (pacing vs. completability), same underlying tool (delay/gate existence in authoritative state, not in the renderer).
- Rule of thumb: When a "this feels too sudden/instant" complaint is about something with GAMEPLAY stakes (can hurt/be hurt/be interacted with) rather than pure decoration, check whether the fix needs to change WHEN the entity exists in authoritative state, not just how its sprite is drawn — a renderer-only tween/fade is only sufficient when the thing being eased in truly has no mechanical effect yet; otherwise it's cosmetic authority that contradicts the sim's real state, which reads as more broken than an instant pop, not less.

## E5 — waitingcity verify mechanic extension rewards real variance
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [gamedev][design]
- What: Before extending a new mechanic to cover an edge case it wasn't designed for, check whether the extension would actually respond to something the player did differently — or whether it would just fire identically for every player, relabeling an existing guaranteed event under the new mechanic's name.
- Why built: Deciding whether the new per-kind "confidence" system (regular guards reveal hp/power/AI-state once `player.intel[kind]` — built from real wins AND losses against that kind — clears a threshold) should be generalized to the Warden boss fight too, for consistency.
- Design: The Warden already fires a `boss_taunted` event at 50% hp, unconditionally, for every player, every playthrough. The tempting move was to key a "confidence" reveal off that event, so the boss would "feel" consistent with the new system.
- Got right: Asked the falsifying question directly — does this actually vary by anything the player did, or does it fire the same for everyone regardless of how the fight is going? — before writing the code, not after playtesting revealed the reveal felt hollow.
- Where/why it failed: `boss_taunted` fires at a fixed hp threshold with no variance across playthroughs; a player who is barely surviving and a player crushing the fight both hit it identically. Framing that as "confidence earned through the fight" would be pure relabeling — the underlying event carries zero signal about player skill or exposure, unlike the regular guards' `player.intel[kind]`, which genuinely differs based on how many real encounters (wins or losses) the player has actually had.
- Fix: Did not extend the mechanic to the boss. Kept it on the original flat perception-stat gate as a named, documented exception (see the companion entry on the respawn discovery that reinforced this call) rather than forcing false consistency.
- Why this fix: A reveal mechanic's entire point is to reward something the player actually did. Wrapping a new name around a guaranteed, invariant event doesn't reward anything — it just adds a layer of narrative gloss that a careful player (or a future maintainer reading the code) would see through immediately.
- Rule of thumb: Before generalizing mechanic M (built to respond to real variance in condition C) to cover new case X, check whether X actually produces variance in C, or whether X's trigger is a fixed, guaranteed event every player hits identically. If the latter, keep X on its old mechanism as a deliberate, named exception — don't force uniformity for its own sake.

## E3 — wrongsky batch canvas filter not per drawcall
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-wrong-sky
- Tags: [gamedev][rendering][canvas][perf]
- What: Setting Canvas 2D `ctx.filter` (e.g. `grayscale`/`hue-rotate`) individually before EVERY `drawImage` call, instead of once per frame on a pre-composited layer, causes catastrophic per-frame cost that scales with entity/tile count — a single `render()` call measured at 19,321.6ms (vs. a normal ~5-8ms) once enough sprites were on screen for the filter to apply to hundreds of draws.
- Why built: The game's presentation reveals sprite layers (`world`/`enemies`/`player`) one quest at a time; until each layer is "restored," its sprites render in grayscale (and briefly hue-cycle through a ~7s "kaleidoscope" on the light reveal). The natural-looking implementation set `ctx.filter = filter` and drew each ground tile, wall, entity, and projectile with that filter active, one `drawImage` per game object per frame.
- Design: World-space rendering already ran through a per-frame loop building a `drawables` list (destructibles/pickups/wells/npcs/enemies/player), each closure doing its own `ctx.fillStyle`/`drawPixelSprite` calls directly against the visible canvas context under a shared camera transform.
- Got right: The VISUAL result (uniform grayscale-until-restored, animated hue-cycle reveal) was correct and matched spec exactly — this was a pure performance defect, not a correctness one. It stayed invisible early on because during quest 1 (light off, world/enemies still flat, few sprites) the draw count per frame was low enough that per-call filtering was cheap; only once `visual.world`/`visual.enemies` sprites were active (quests 2-4, hundreds of tile+entity `drawPixelSprite` calls/frame, all pre-light so still filtered) did the cost become visible, explaining the user's precise report ("tremendous lag during quest 2 through 4, it gets better at 5" — quest 5 is post-light, filter becomes the cheap `'none'`).
- Where/why it failed: Canvas 2D's `ctx.filter` is not a cheap state flag — each draw call with a non-`'none'` filter forces the browser to rasterize an intermediate filtered surface for that call. Applying it hundreds of times a frame (once per ground tile, once per wall, once per entity, once per projectile) multiplies that rasterization cost by the draw count instead of paying it once. Found via direct Playwright profiling: dispatched the sim to the "light off, world+player+enemies revealed" state and timed individual `render()` calls with `performance.now()`, isolating the transition into/out of that state to bracket the spike precisely (an earlier profiling attempt had an off-by-one that measured the SAME state twice and missed the effect entirely — had to explicitly stop the dispatch loop strictly before the state-changing command to isolate it).
- Fix: Introduced an offscreen `worldLayer` canvas the same size as the visible canvas. All ground/wall/entity/projectile drawing for the frame targets that buffer's context (`wctx`), UNFILTERED (`filter: 'none'` / omitted), under the same camera transform as the visible canvas. After all world-space content is drawn, do exactly ONE `ctx.drawImage(worldLayer, 0, 0)` with `ctx.filter` set to the frame's real filter, then reset it to `'none'`. Lighting/shadowcasting darkness overlay and the on-top wall redraw (never the bottleneck — plain `fillRect`, and only a handful of on-screen wall tiles) stay directly on the visible canvas, drawn after the composite.
- Why this fix: The filter only needs to apply to the FINAL composited pixels once — grouping every world-space draw into one unfiltered buffer and filtering the single resulting blit produces the identical visual output (verified: golden sim fingerprint unchanged, browser e2e pixel-behavior unchanged) for O(1) filter applications per frame instead of O(draw calls).
- Rule of thumb: In Canvas 2D, never set `ctx.filter` to a non-`'none'` value before each of many small `drawImage`/fill calls in a loop — composite the whole filtered region into an offscreen buffer first (unfiltered draws), then apply the filter to ONE blit of that buffer. This generalizes past grayscale/hue-rotate to any CSS filter (blur, drop-shadow, etc.) and any renderer that draws a scene procedurally tile-by-tile/entity-by-entity. A frame-time regression that only appears once entity/tile COUNT crosses some threshold (rather than being present from the start) is a strong signal to profile per-draw-call overhead specifically, not just "general slowness."

- Link: [redacted]

## E4 — wrongsky exclusive multi offer must revoke siblings
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-wrong-sky
- Tags: [gamedev][quest][state]
- What: Offering several mutually-exclusive choices at once (a "pick one of N" batch) does NOT become exclusive just because a human reads it that way — the accept/select handler must explicitly withdraw the other pending offers in the same batch, or a player (or a replayed sequence of commands) can accept more than one.
- Why built: A branching "philosophical choice" quest chain offers 3 quests simultaneously (reveal-player/world/enemies) via one combined event; the fiction frames accepting any one as an exclusive pick, narrowing to 2 remaining, then 1, as each completes.
- Design: `ACCEPT_QUEST` validates the target quest was offered (`state.quests.offered[q]`) and activates it. The intended design (stated in the code's own comment) was that accepting one quest "withdraws sibling offers from the same batch."
- Got right: The comment correctly stated the INTENT; the surrounding systems (facet-matching, reveal-order tracking) were built assuming that intent held.
- Where/why it failed: The actual code only ever did `delete state.quests.offered[q]` for the ACCEPTED quest — it never touched the other two still-`offered=1` entries from the same TALK batch. Found by an adversarial review pass, not by the existing test suite: every test and the scripted demo always re-TALK'd between each accept (which recomputes the whole offered set from scratch and incidentally produces the right-looking result), so the actual defect — accepting `reveal-player` then immediately, without re-talking, also being able to accept `reveal-world` and `reveal-enemies` in the same "turn" — was never exercised. It didn't crash, corrupt state, or double-credit anything (the underlying facet-matching logic was independently correct for whichever quests happened to be active), but it silently broke the "exclusive pick" premise the whole branching-narrative feature depends on.
- Fix: On accept, delete every OTHER still-offered quest with the same `giver`, not just the one being accepted. The next `TALK` still recomputes the correct remaining offerable set from scratch (active/completed/requires), so nothing legitimately eligible is lost — only the stale batch is cleared.
- Why this fix: The invariant ("this batch of offers is exclusive") needs to be enforced by the command that consumes the choice, not left as an assumption the surrounding narrative and test scripts happen not to violate. A test that never deviates from the "expected" interaction order (talk → accept → talk → accept → ...) cannot distinguish "the design is enforced" from "the design is merely never violated by this script."
- Rule of thumb: When N options are offered together as an exclusive choice, the code that handles picking ONE must explicitly clear the others in that same command — don't rely on the caller (a scripted demo, typical play) to never test the gap. Write at least one test that deliberately accepts a second sibling WITHOUT the "expected" re-offer step in between, specifically to probe whether exclusivity is enforced by the state machine or just by convention.

## E5 — wrongsky existence check not truthy for magnitude maps
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-wrong-sky
- Tags: [state][gamedev]
- What: A sparse map whose VALUES carry a magnitude that can legitimately be 0 must be checked by key existence (`in` / `hasOwnProperty`), never by truthiness (`if (map[key])`) — 0 is a valid, meaningful value, not "absent."
- Why built: `region.blocked` changed from a boolean-flag map (`{ "x,y": 1 }`) to an opacity map (`{ "x,y": 0-100 }`) so a single structure could drive both movement collision (existence: is this tile blocked at all) and a new line-of-sight/shadowcasting system (magnitude: how strongly does it occlude). The two consumers have genuinely different questions to ask of the same map.
- Design: Collision (`MOVE`'s blocked-tile check) is supposed to be existence-based per the code's own stated design ("ANY entry blocks MOVE regardless of the opacity number's magnitude") — a 0-opacity tile (a legitimate future case: a fully-transparent-but-solid wall, e.g. an invisible barrier) should still block movement; opacity only matters to the separate visibility calculation.
- Got right: The visibility/shadowcasting side of the codebase (`visibility.js`) was written with magnitude in mind from the start and handles 0 opacity correctly (multiplying clarity by `(100-0)/100 = 1`, a no-op).
- Where/why it failed: The MOVE collision check kept its ORIGINAL truthy check (`if (state.region.blocked[key])`) from when the map stored plain `1`s. Once the map started storing real opacity integers, a tile authored with opacity `0` would evaluate falsy and silently stop blocking movement — the exact case the opacity model was explicitly designed to support (a solid-but-invisible object) breaks the one guarantee (solid = blocks movement) that's supposed to be unconditional. Not triggered by current content (every authored blocked tile happens to use opacity 100), so it shipped invisibly; found by an adversarial code-review pass specifically asked to probe the opacity model's edge cases, not by any test failure.
- Fix: Changed the collision check to `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(state.region.blocked, key)` (existence), leaving the opacity VALUE itself untouched for the visibility system to read separately. Added a regression test that authors a 0-opacity blocked tile and asserts it still blocks movement — the previous test suite only ever exercised opacity-100 tiles, so it couldn't have caught this.
- Why this fix: The two consumers (collision, visibility) ask genuinely different questions of the same map — "does this key exist" vs "what's the number" — and conflating them into one truthy check works only by coincidence, for exactly as long as nobody authors the value the design explicitly anticipates.
- Rule of thumb: When a map's value carries a MAGNITUDE and 0 is a legitimate value (opacity, a modifier, a count, a discount), never gate behavior on the value's truthiness — gate on whether the KEY exists. Truthy-checking a magnitude map is a landmine that specifically detonates on the most boundary-adjacent, most "should obviously work" input (zero) — and a test suite built only on non-zero example data will never trip it. When reviewing or writing tests for any sparse map keyed by "is this present," deliberately add a zero-magnitude case.

## E6 — wrongsky ui hint must share source with input check
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-wrong-sky
- Tags: [input][ui][gamedev]
- What: A displayed key/button hint and the input-handling code it describes must derive from the SAME source of truth — an authored hint field that isn't literally what the handler checks will silently drift the moment the two are written by different reasoning.
- Why built: Single-button "hold to dismiss" dialogs use a dedicated press-and-hold on `blast` (a different physical button than confirm/attack, chosen specifically because confirm and attack shared a gamepad button and caused accidental dismissal during combat). The modal's displayed hint used a per-option `hintAction` field (`'confirm'` for most dialogs, `'cancel'` for one), independently authored when each modal was constructed.
- Design: The renderer draws `withHint(device, opts[0].hintAction || 'blast', ...)` — reading the AUTHORED field first, falling back to `'blast'` only if the field was absent.
- Got right: The actual dismiss LOGIC (`handleModal`'s length-1 branch) was written correctly and consistently checks `blastHeld` alone — the mechanism itself has no bug.
- Where/why it failed: Every dialog-constructing call site set `hintAction` to whatever seemed locally sensible for a "Continue"/"Close" button (`'confirm'` or `'cancel'`) — a reasonable-looking choice made independently of, and never cross-checked against, what the ONE handler that consumes all single-button modals actually reads. The result: every intro/NPC/boss/defeat/finale dialog in the game displayed "Hold to Continue (Enter)" or "(A)" — a real, existing device-labels.js hint for the WRONG action — while the code only ever responded to holding blast (K / gamepad button 2). A keyboard- or gamepad-only player with no pointer device, trusting the on-screen text, would hold the shown button indefinitely with the progress bar visibly stuck at 0%. Compounding the miss: `device-labels.js` had no `'blast'` entry at all, so even the fallback path was broken.
- Fix: The renderer's single-option branch now hardcodes `'blast'` as the hint action, ignoring any `hintAction` an option happens to carry (that field is only meaningful for the 2+-option navigable branch, where every option really is confirmed the same way). Added the missing `blast` entries to `device-labels.js`.
- Why this fix: For a UI element with exactly one possible interaction, there is exactly one correct hint, and it should be a structural fact of that code path (hardcoded to match the one handler), not a per-caller-authored field that has to happen to agree with logic living in a different function entirely.
- Rule of thumb: When a hint/help string and the behavior it describes are written in two different places (a data field vs. an event handler), they can only be kept honest by construction — derive the hint from whatever single source the handler itself reads, or hardcode it to match a hardcoded handler, rather than letting each call site author its own guess. An independent adversarial review (not the unit test suite, which only checks the mechanism works when the RIGHT button is held) is what caught this — a test that "holds blast and confirms dismissal" cannot detect that the LABEL told the player to hold a different button.

## E6 — waitingcity ontouchstart false negatives on real touch hardware
- Date: 2026-07-09
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [input][browser][touch][mobile]
- What: `'ontouchstart' in window` alone is not a reliable touch-capability detector — it false-negatives on real touch hardware, most notably iPadOS Safari's default desktop-site mode, which drops `ontouchstart` entirely despite the device being fully touch-capable. Any UI default gated on that single check (e.g. "show on-screen touch controls by default on a touch device") silently fails on those devices.
- Why built: A player reported the on-screen d-pad/action buttons never appeared by default. `input.js`'s `hasTouch` was computed as `'ontouchstart' in window` — a legacy feature-detect this codebase's device-adaptive-UI kernel (`../ideas/idea-repository.md`, the "detect the active input device... display the controls of the device actually in their hands" fragment) depends on entirely: `view.device` only ever defaults to `'touch'` when `input.hasTouch` is true.
- Design: `input.hasTouch` is computed once at `makeInput()` construction and consumed downstream in `game.js`/`title.js` as `input.hasTouch && device === 'keyboard' ? 'touch' : device` — a sentinel pattern meaning "no real input signal has been seen yet AND this device supports touch, so default to touch." The entire mechanism is gated on that one boolean being correct at construction time.
- Got right: Didn't assume the bug report meant "the touch-zone hit-testing is broken" (which would need debugging actual event handlers) — reproduced the specific "not shown BY DEFAULT" framing first, which pointed straight at the one-time `hasTouch` capability check rather than any per-frame logic.
- Where/why it failed: Directly reproduced by simulating a touch-capable-but-no-ontouchstart environment (`navigator.maxTouchPoints = 5`, `window.ontouchstart` deleted — matching real iPadOS Safari, which reports itself in desktop-site mode by default since iPadOS 13 and drops `ontouchstart` for legacy-site compatibility) and confirming `input.hasTouch` resolved to `false` under that exact condition, before writing any fix — the on-screen controls never had a chance to appear because the capability check that gates them was already wrong at construction.
- Fix: `hasTouch: 'ontouchstart' in window || (navigator.maxTouchPoints || 0) > 0` — OR in `navigator.maxTouchPoints`, the more reliable modern touch-capability signal, instead of replacing `ontouchstart` outright (some older/embedded WebViews may only expose the legacy property).
- Why this fix: Confirmed the same simulated environment now resolves `hasTouch: true`, and confirmed live (via a real playthrough screenshot) that the on-screen pad/buttons render by default under it — the fix targets exactly the reproduced failure, not a broader rewrite of the input-detection layer.
- Rule of thumb: Never gate a UI default on `'ontouchstart' in window` alone — it is a legacy check known to false-negative on real touch hardware (iPadOS Safari's default desktop-site mode being the most common real-world case). OR it with `navigator.maxTouchPoints > 0`. More generally: when a bug report says a UI element "doesn't show by DEFAULT" (vs. "doesn't respond to my input"), suspect a one-time capability/feature-detect computed at construction before any real interaction, not per-frame event-handling logic — and reproduce it by directly faking the platform-detection signals in a test environment rather than assuming a specific physical device is required to repro.

## E7 — waitingcity world scale must fit shorter screen axis
- Date: 2026-07-09
- Project: pappydapimp69/the-game-the-waiting-city
- Tags: [gamedev][rendering][mobile]
- What: A top-down camera's world-to-screen scale formula that fits N tiles along canvas HEIGHT unconditionally is a landscape-only assumption (H is the constraining/shorter screen dimension) — it silently breaks on any portrait screen, where H is the LARGER raw-pixel dimension, blowing the zoom level way up and leaving only a sliver of width on screen.
- Why built: A player reported "the screen scale is wrong" on mobile. `renderer.js` computed `const scale = H / (16 * TILE)` — fit exactly 16 tiles of world vertically, always, regardless of device.
- Design: The backing canvas store tracks `window.innerWidth/innerHeight × devicePixelRatio` (main.js's resize()), so on a real phone in portrait the canvas is often TALLER in raw pixels than any desktop browser window (e.g. 780×1688 at DPR 2, vs. a typical desktop window's 1280×720). The scale formula only ever looked at H, with the unstated assumption baked in at authoring time (on a desktop landscape window, H is always the smaller dimension, so "fit 16 tiles to H" reads as "fit 16 tiles to whichever dimension is shorter" — that equivalence quietly stops holding on portrait).
- Got right: Traced the actual math instead of guessing — computed what `scale` and the resulting visible-tile-count would be for a real portrait viewport (390×844 CSS, DPR 3 → 780×1688 canvas backing store) versus the assumed-landscape case, and confirmed the discrepancy (portrait: ~7.4 tiles of width visible vs. the intended 16) before touching code.
- Where/why it failed: `scale = H / (16*TILE)` with H=1688 gives scale≈4.4 — over double the ~2.0 scale a similarly-sized desktop window would produce (H≈800). Since `viewWpx = W / scale`, a larger scale directly shrinks the visible width; on portrait, W is already small (780px) and scale is ALSO inflated, compounding into a severely cropped, over-zoomed field of view.
- Fix: `const scale = Math.min(W, H) / (16 * TILE)` — fit 16 tiles along whichever screen dimension is actually shorter. On desktop/landscape this is a no-op (H was already the smaller side). On portrait it correctly locks the SHORT axis (now W) to 16 tiles and lets the long axis (H) show proportionally more world, instead of the reverse.
- Why this fix: Preserves the exact existing desktop/landscape experience (verified via a before/after screenshot — pixel-identical framing) while fixing portrait by construction by generalizing "fit the constraining dimension" from its silently-landscape-only special case to any aspect ratio.
- Rule of thumb: A camera/viewport scale formula authored and only ever tested on a landscape desktop window that references ONE fixed screen dimension ("fit N units to H" or "to W") is implicitly encoding an aspect-ratio assumption, not a general rule — replace the single dimension with `Math.min(W, H)` (or the dimension the design actually wants to bound) before shipping to any device where portrait is possible, and verify with an actual portrait-viewport screenshot, not just a resized desktop window (a resized desktop window is still landscape-biased and won't surface this).
